title: Checkout timeouts caused by a slow dependency
content: Repeated request timeouts to a single downstream service usually indicate dependency latency or saturation rather than an application-only bug. Confirm whether retries are amplifying load and whether latency rises before error volume spikes.

title: Database pool exhaustion pattern
content: Connection pool exhaustion often shows up as queued requests, timeout errors, and a sudden increase in active sessions. Validate pool usage, slow query volume, and whether recent traffic or retry storms pushed concurrency past configured limits.

title: Dependency outage with connection refusal
content: Connection refused, upstream 503 responses, and circuit breaker openings usually point to a dependency outage or an unreachable upstream. Check dependency health, network reachability, and whether fallback logic is masking the blast radius.

title: Memory pressure before restarts
content: Out-of-memory incidents often begin with increasing latency, allocator failures, or pod restarts before the service becomes unavailable. Review heap growth, container memory limits, and whether one workload shape triggers abnormal allocation spikes.

title: Primary database unavailable
content: Database incidents with connection resets, failover messages, or read-only errors can indicate an unavailable primary or incomplete failover. Confirm database role state, replication lag, and whether clients are pinned to an old endpoint.

title: Retry storm feedback loop
content: Aggressive retries can turn a minor latency issue into a sustained incident. If timeout errors, queue depth, and request volume climb together, inspect retry budgets, exponential backoff settings, and any fan-out path multiplying traffic.

title: Service discovery and DNS failures
content: Transient name resolution errors or frequent endpoint changes often point to DNS or service discovery instability. Validate resolver health, recent service registration changes, and whether cached endpoints differ across callers.

title: Storage saturation driving database latency
content: When database latency rises alongside disk or IOPS saturation, the likely issue is infrastructure contention rather than only application concurrency. Check storage metrics, checkpoint activity, and whether background maintenance jobs overlap with peak traffic.

title: Database deadlocks under concurrent writes
content: Deadlock errors during bursts of write traffic usually mean two or more transaction paths are locking rows in inconsistent order. Check recent schema changes, transaction boundaries, and whether retries are inflating lock contention.

title: Cache dependency timeout pattern
content: Redis or cache timeouts often produce application latency spikes before outright failures. Validate network latency to the cache tier, command volume, eviction pressure, and whether callers are falling back to the primary database.

title: Thread pool starvation and latency
content: If request latency increases while worker threads remain fully occupied, thread pool starvation may be blocking progress. Check synchronous calls in request paths, queue depth, and whether one downstream dependency is holding threads open too long.

title: Kubernetes OOMKilled restart sequence
content: Pods that repeatedly restart with OOMKilled status usually exceed container memory limits rather than failing application health checks first. Confirm restart count, memory RSS growth, and whether new traffic patterns or payload sizes changed recently.

title: Expired certificate on ingress or edge
content: TLS handshake failures and certificate expired errors at the edge indicate expired or mis-rotated certificates. Verify the certificate validity window, listener configuration, and whether renewal automation ran successfully.

title: Dependency rate limiting under burst traffic
content: A surge of 429 responses from one downstream API usually means the caller exceeded a dependency limit. Check request burst size, retry behavior, client-side throttling, and whether new traffic was shifted to the same dependency path.

title: Queue backlog and consumer lag
content: When queue depth increases faster than consumers can process work, backlog and end-to-end latency grow together. Validate consumer throughput, poison messages, and whether one partition or topic is blocking progress.

title: Gateway 504 caused by downstream saturation
content: Repeated 504 responses at the API gateway often reflect latency in one downstream service rather than gateway misbehavior. Correlate gateway timing with dependency latency, retry traffic, and backend saturation signals.

title: Replication lag causing stale or inconsistent reads
content: If reads become stale or inconsistent after failover or heavy writes, replication lag may be the real issue. Check replica freshness, lag metrics, and whether the application assumes immediate read-after-write consistency.

title: Disk full errors in database systems
content: Write failures, checkpoint errors, or sudden read-only behavior can happen when a database node runs out of disk. Verify free space, WAL growth, backup retention, and recent maintenance jobs that increased storage use.

title: Partner API schema or contract change
content: A burst of parse errors or unexpected field validation failures from a single partner integration can mean the partner changed response shape or schema. Compare recent payload samples and confirm whether contract versioning changed without notice.

title: Circuit breaker opening after dependency instability
content: Frequent circuit breaker openings usually follow elevated dependency latency or failure rates. Check the upstream error ratio, breaker thresholds, and whether the system is oscillating between partial recovery and renewed overload.

title: Long-lived process memory leak
content: When memory usage climbs steadily across hours or days and restarts temporarily reset the problem, a memory leak or unbounded cache is likely. Review object growth patterns, cache eviction policy, and request paths with retained state.

title: Regional network partition symptoms
content: Simultaneous connection timeouts and refusals across multiple dependencies in one region can indicate a network partition or routing issue rather than independent service failures. Compare impact by region and check control plane or VPC networking events.

title: Postgres authentication failures
content: Password authentication failed or role login denied errors usually point to rotated credentials, revoked users, or mismatched secrets rather than pool exhaustion. Validate the configured database user, recent secret changes, and whether all clients reloaded updated credentials.

title: Mongo server selection timeout
content: Mongo server selection timeout errors generally indicate that the client cannot find a writable primary or healthy replica set member. Check replica set state, DNS resolution, TLS settings, and whether the application is pinned to stale topology information.

title: Writes sent to read-only replica
content: Read-only transaction errors in PostgreSQL usually mean application writes were routed to a replica instead of the primary. Validate connection routing, failover state, and whether a stale service endpoint still targets a read-only node.

title: Statement timeout from slow aggregation queries
content: Statement timeout errors during reporting or analytics usually point to inefficient scans, missing indexes, or insufficient capacity for heavy aggregation. Review the specific query plan, recent data growth, and whether maintenance lag increased table bloat.

title: Redis connection refused
content: Redis connection refused on port 6379 usually indicates a cache outage, listener problem, or network path failure rather than generic timeout behavior. Validate Redis health, listener bindings, failover state, and whether reconnect loops are exhausting clients.

title: TLS or token failures caused by clock skew
content: JWT not yet valid, handshake validity window failures, and certificate validation errors can happen when node clocks drift apart. Check NTP synchronization, timezone configuration, and whether failures correlate to a subset of hosts or zones.

title: Filesystem permission regression after policy change
content: Permission denied while writing to a local mount usually indicates a policy, IAM, or filesystem ownership regression rather than an external dependency outage. Validate recent policy changes, service account mappings, and mount ownership on the affected hosts.

title: Invalid runtime configuration after deploy
content: Missing required configuration keys immediately after deployment usually indicate a release or environment regression. Check the new deployment manifest, configuration injection path, and whether rollback restores the missing setting.

title: Application host out of disk space
content: No space left on device on an application host usually points to local disk exhaustion rather than memory or database failure. Check filesystem utilization, retention policies, spool growth, and whether logs or temporary files expanded unexpectedly.

title: Mixed partner timeout and database contention signals
content: When partner feed timeouts appear alongside transient database serialization failures, the safest interpretation is mixed evidence rather than a single confident root cause. Separate remote dependency timing from local database contention before committing to one explanation.

title: Mixed CPU saturation and timeout signals
content: If deadline misses happen at the same time as sustained CPU saturation, the timeout may be local compute contention rather than a downstream dependency. Compare worker CPU, queue depth, and outbound dependency latency before attributing the incident.

title: apiserver: tolerate APF header race with timeout handler in priority-and-fairness tests
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
 

What this PR does / why we need it:
TestPriorityAndFairnessWithPanicRecoveryAndTimeoutFilter assumes that the API Priority and Fairness (APF) filter always has an opportunity to attach FlowSchema and PriorityLevel headers to the response.

However, when a request times out, the timeout handler may commit the response before APF attaches its headers. In that case header writes become best-effort and the expected headers may not appear in the response.

title: apiserver: tolerate APF header race with timeout handler in priority-and-fairness tests
content:
This introduces a race between the timeout handler and APF header attachment in the test environment, which can surface as intermittent test failures.

This PR updates the test helper (headerMatcher.inspect) to tolerate cases where the request context is already done or the response has already been committed, since APF headers may legitimately be absent in those scenarios.
Which issue(s) this PR is related to: #135941
 
 (issue in a different repository)

title: apiserver: tolerate APF header race with timeout handler in priority-and-fairness tests
content:
(issue in a different repository)
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/ 

If there is no associated issue, then write "N/A".
-->

Special notes for your reviewer:
The change only relaxes header assertions in the test helper when the request context has already been cancelled or the response has been committed. In those situations header writes from inner handlers are best-effort and may not be present in the response.

title: apiserver: tolerate APF header race with timeout handler in priority-and-fairness tests
content:
This keeps the test focused on validating APF behavior without assuming deterministic ordering between timeout handling and APF header writes.

title: [evented pleg]: using real-time container events for pod state determination
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug

Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
Fixes #124704 #121003

Special notes for your reviewer:
The purpose of this PR is to resolve #124704

I have carefully introduced the causes of this problem and various solutions we've attempted to resolve it in https://docs.google.com/document/d/1TPrY56q9MNW8r1FuzKDFkBBhOjQ0hqi7wJAbIP1O-4g/

title: [evented pleg]: using real-time container events for pod state determination
content: This PR proposes that during its execution cycle, should directly use the latest events reported by the container runtime as the current Pod state. If we can confirm that the events reported by the container runtime are reliable and up-to-date, we can stop relying on timestamps to determine whether the state is current

title: [evented pleg]: using real-time container events for pod state determination
content: . In both Generic PLEG and the current Evented PLEG, if the container state does not change, the timestamps in the cache are still updated unnecessarily (every 5 seconds and 1 second, respectively). This shows that timestamp-based checks are meaningless when the container state remains unchanged

title: [evented pleg]: using real-time container events for pod state determination
content: The current container lifecycle code is not yet ready to directly receive status reported by the container runtime. To address this issue:

title: [evented pleg]: using real-time container events for pod state determination
content: 1. The EventedPLEG needs to filter out sandbox creation and deletion events. This is because the container runtime reports events for sandbox containers and regular containers in the same way, which the kubelet has never handled before

title: [evented pleg]: using real-time container events for pod state determination
content: . Therefore, we have added an additional condition to ensure that creation events for sandbox containers do not trigger lifecycle processing before the creation of regular containers, and deletion events for sandbox containers do not trigger lifecycle processing after the deletion of regular containers.

title: [evented pleg]: using real-time container events for pod state determination
content:
2. If syncPod needs to call the container runtime's API in any form to create/delete/update containers, we will actively list the container status after syncPod completes. This ensures the cache status remains synchronized with the latest container state and prevents subsequent EventedPLEG events from updating the cache and compromising its consistency.

title: EKS 1.34  DaemonSet Controller created a lot of pod during node deletion
content:
What happened?

Hello,

Since upgrading our cluster to Kubernetes version 1.34, we have encountered destructive behavior in system components during node decommission (scale-down/termination). The issue manifests as an infinite pod recreation cycle, causing significant overhead on the API server.

title: EKS 1.34  DaemonSet Controller created a lot of pod during node deletion
content:
Issue Description:
When a node initiates the shutdown sequence, the Kubelet correctly begins rejecting pod execution. However, high-level controllers (specifically the DaemonSet Controller) do not interpret this state as final and continuously attempt to reschedule pods back onto the same shutting-down node.

Observed Symptoms:

1)Massive Pod Rejections: Pods are terminated or rejected with the error: Pod was rejected: Pod was rejected as the node is shutting down.

title: EKS 1.34  DaemonSet Controller created a lot of pod during node deletion
content:
2)Scheduler Loop: The scheduler assigns a pod to the node -> Kubelet rejects it -> The Controller detects the missing pod and triggers a new request -> The Scheduler picks the same node again.

3)Affected System Components (stuck in Pending):

istio-cni-node, ztunnel

aws-node, kube-proxy

ebs-csi-node, efs-csi-node, s3-csi-node

eks-node-monitoring-agent, eks-pod-identity-agent

title: EKS 1.34  DaemonSet Controller created a lot of pod during node deletion
content:
eks-node-monitoring-agent, eks-pod-identity-agent

4)ztunnel Flapping: We are observing a critical anomaly where a single ztunnel pod goes through the "Create-Reject-Delete" cycle hundreds of times during a single node's removal. This creates parasitic load on etcd, the kube-apiserver, and Istio control plane.

Technical Context:

K8s Version: 1.34

title: EKS 1.34  DaemonSet Controller created a lot of pod during node deletion
content:
Technical Context:

K8s Version: 1.34

Feature involved: Graceful Node Shutdown. It appears there is a regression in how Node Taints and Lifecycle states are synchronized between the Kubelet and the Scheduler in v1.34.

Impact: System pods remain in Pending indefinitely while the node is dying, preventing clean cluster scaling.

We request your assistance with the following:

Confirm if this is a known regression in the v1.34 Node Lifecycle / DaemonSet logic.

title: EKS 1.34  DaemonSet Controller created a lot of pod during node deletion
content:
Provide recommendations for NodeLifecycleController or GracefulNodeShutdown configurations to prevent the Scheduler from targeting nodes in a shutdown state.

Advise if there is a temporary patch or a specific Feature Gate to resolve this race condition.

Best regards,

What did you expect to happen?

In EKS 1.33 we didn't have such problem with cretion of 300 daemon pod during node deletion.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

title: EKS 1.34  DaemonSet Controller created a lot of pod during node deletion
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Please try to delete node with daemon set pods ztunnel

Anything else we need to know?

No response

kubectl version
Client Version: v1.33.0
Kustomize Version: v5.6.0
Server Version: v1.34.4-eks-3a10415

title: cri-client: use passthrough resolver for socket endpoints
content:
grpc defaults to the DNS resolver for bare dial targets. For CRI socket endpoints, GetAddressAndDialer returns a socket path plus a custom dialer, but passing the bare path to grpc.DialContext still lets grpc resolve the target first.

found when trying to use the latest 1.36 beta.0 in containerd in a WIP PR:
https://github.com/containerd/containerd/pull/13078

title: cri-client: use passthrough resolver for socket endpoints
content:
https://github.com/containerd/containerd/pull/13078

That breaks unix socket clients with errors like "name resolver error: produced zero addresses" before the custom dialer ever sees the raw path. Use the passthrough resolver for socket-style addresses so the runtime and image clients hand the original endpoint directly to the custom dialer.

Add a regression test for unix sockets, Windows named pipes, and TCP addresses.

title: DRA: partial prepare claims with different drivers can leave Pod stuck without rollback
content:
What happened?

When a Pod depends on multiple ResourceClaims managed by different DRA drivers, prepare can succeed for one claim and fail for another.

In that case:
- one driver has already prepared its device and considers the claim successful
- another driver fails
- the Pod gets stuck in FailedPrepareDynamicResources
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
- no rollback happens for the driver that already succeeded

title: DRA: partial prepare claims with different drivers can leave Pod stuck without rollback
content:
This can leave the Pod in a failed state while some devices remain prepared / reserved even though the Pod will never start.

What did you expect to happen?

If any required claim fails during prepare, Kubernetes should eventually trigger cleanup for claims that were already successfully prepared for the same Pod.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

title: DRA: partial prepare claims with different drivers can leave Pod stuck without rollback
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Run two different DRA drivers, each managing a different resource.
2. Create a Pod that requires both resources through separate claims.
3. Let prepare succeed for claim A and fail for claim B.
4. Observe that the Pod ends up in FailedPrepareDynamicResources, but claim A remains prepared.

Anything else we need to know?

title: DRA: partial prepare claims with different drivers can leave Pod stuck without rollback
content:
Anything else we need to know?

Depending on the driver implementation, this can effectively strand capacity and look like a device leak until some separate reconciliation cleans it up.

title: kubelet/cm: fix cgroup v2 memory.min calculation for Guaranteed QoS
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR fixes a bug in setMemoryQoS (pkg/kubelet/cm/qoscontainermanager_linux.go) where memory.min for Guaranteed pods incorrectly includes Burstable requests.

title: kubelet/cm: fix cgroup v2 memory.min calculation for Guaranteed QoS
content:
Under the cgroup v2 hierarchy, kubepods-guaranteed and kubepods-burstable are siblings. Summing their requests for the Guaranteed cgroup artificially inflates its memory.min (causing memory hoarding) and leaves the Burstable sibling without effective parent-level soft protection.

The Fix:

kubepods-guaranteed memory.min now strictly equals Guaranteed requests.

kubepods-burstable memory.low strictly equals Burstable requests.

title: kubelet/cm: fix cgroup v2 memory.min calculation for Guaranteed QoS
content:
kubepods-burstable memory.low strictly equals Burstable requests.

The root kubepods cgroup now sets an overarching memory.low (G+B) to protect both siblings.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Related to the recent MemoryQoS refactor in #137889.

Special notes for your reviewer:
/sig node

Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

title: kubelet/cm: fix cgroup v2 memory.min calculation for Guaranteed QoS
content:
/sig node

Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
Fixes a cgroup v2 MemoryQoS bug where the Guaranteed cgroup incorrectly reserved memory requested by Burstable pods, causing memory hoarding and ineffective soft protections under node pressure.

title: CVE-2026-3864: CSI Driver for NFS path traversal via subDir may delete unintended directories on the NFS server
content:
CVSS Rating: 
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:H/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:H — Medium (6.5)

title: CVE-2026-3864: CSI Driver for NFS path traversal via subDir may delete unintended directories on the NFS server
content: A vulnerability was discovered in the Kubernetes CSI Driver for NFS where the subDir parameter in volume identifiers was insufficiently validated. Attackers with the ability to create PersistentVolumes referencing the NFS CSI driver could craft volume identifiers containing path traversal sequences (../). During volume deletion or cleanup operations, the driver could operate on unintended directories outside the intended managed path within the NFS export.

title: CVE-2026-3864: CSI Driver for NFS path traversal via subDir may delete unintended directories on the NFS server
content:
This may lead to deletion or modification of directories on the NFS server.

Am I vulnerable?

You may be vulnerable if:
You run the CSI Driver for NFS (nfs.csi.k8s.io)
Your cluster allows users to create PersistentVolumes referencing the NFS CSI driver
Your CSI driver version does not validate traversal sequences in the subDir field

Affected Versions
All versions of the CSI Driver for NFS prior to the v4.13.1 release containing the fix for traversal validation are affected.

title: CVE-2026-3864: CSI Driver for NFS path traversal via subDir may delete unintended directories on the NFS server
content:
How do I mitigate this vulnerability?

This issue can be mitigated by:
Upgrading the CSI Driver for NFS to a patched version
Restricting PersistentVolume creation privileges to trusted administrators
Reviewing NFS exports to ensure only intended directories are writable by the driver

As a best practice, untrusted users should not be granted permission to create arbitrary PersistentVolumes referencing external storage drivers.

Fixed Versions

CSI Driver for NFS versions >= v4.13.1

Detection

title: CVE-2026-3864: CSI Driver for NFS path traversal via subDir may delete unintended directories on the NFS server
content:
Fixed Versions

CSI Driver for NFS versions >= v4.13.1

Detection

To determine if your cluster may be affected:
Inspect PersistentVolumes using the NFS CSI driver and review the volumeHandle field.
Look for traversal sequences such as: 
Review CSI controller logs for unexpected directory operations. e.g. “Removing subPath: /tmp/mount-uuid/legitimate/../../../exports/subdir”

If you find evidence that this vulnerability has been exploited, please contact security@kubernetes.io

title: CVE-2026-3864: CSI Driver for NFS path traversal via subDir may delete unintended directories on the NFS server
content:
Acknowledgements

This vulnerability was reported by Shaul Ben Hai, Senior Staff Security Researcher from SentinelOne.

The issue was fixed by the CSI Driver for NFS maintainers and the Kubernetes Security Response Committee. 

Andy Zhang @andyzhangx
Rita Zhang @ritazh

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content:
What happened?

PodMemory metric continued to be available for few minutes after pod was removed from the node.

What did you expect to happen?

PodMemory metric should have been removed from the old pod instance as soon as new pod was destroyed or new pod was created.

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content: From initial understanding, kubelet/cAdvisor housekeepingInterval seems to be the static interval at which housekeeping should occur. If allowDynamicHousekeeping is enabled, cAdvisor determines the actual housekeeping interval based on houseKeepingInterval and maxHousekeepingInterval as shown in manager/container.go#L481C1-L505C2 with some jitter.

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content: Need to understand the behaviour of statsCacheDuration and maxHousekeepingInterval when kubelet is restarted. Does kubelet dump the cache data to disk before the package upgrade?

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content:
Moreover, since these maintenance are happening for kubelet upgrades, does time to upgrade and bootstrap kubelet could lead to additional delay in housekeeping. One more weird observation I could find is, node uuid has changed twice, once while upgrading at 11 AM(which is relevant for this issue) and then again at 2 PM(which is unrelated).

 

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

This was observed during kubernetes upgrade.
Node was drained and kubelet was upgraded.

Anything else we need to know?

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content:
Anything else we need to know?

Context
In case of Kubernetes Upgrade related maintenances, pods are rescheduled or temporarily evicted from the node. It has been observed that the newly formed pod consumes approximately double the memory of the original pod at startup. Multiple alerts are raised due to this as it can violate set alerting thresholds.

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content:
Below is the original query raising PodMemory alert for workload. We can notice the query is granular at pod level.
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
From the graph view, we can see a sudden spike in memory usage before 11 AM when the pod got rescheduled.

 
However, since the above query doesn't show the pod reschedule, we can fix it by adding pod uid into the query.

title: Incorrect PodMemory usage after container termination
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
Now, the below graph clearly shows data for earlier instance of pod grocery-2-0 is available till 11:10, well into the startup of new instances of pod grocery-2-0. This contributes to the overall memory usage since we are doing a join query with granularity of pod labels. You can also see that once the metrics for older instance stop relaying, the overall memory usage for pod grocery-2-0 drops down.

title: Remove reconcilePodMemoryProtection that resets pod cgroup values on systemd
content:
What type of PR is this?
 /kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

Removes which called cgroupManager.Update() on every pod cgroup with Unified: {"memory.min": "0", "memory.low":"0"}. On the systemd cgroup driver, this triggers SetUnitProperties via dbus which resets other pod cgroup properties (MemoryMax, CPUWeight, CPUQuota) to defaults, breaking InPlacePodLevelResourcesVerticalScaling and summary_test.

title: Remove reconcilePodMemoryProtection that resets pod cgroup values on systemd
content:
I think the per-pod cleanup is unnecessary as cgroup v2 memory protection is hierarchical, so clearing the parent QoS-class cgroup's memory.low=0 (which setMemoryQoS still does) makes all child pod values functionally ineffective regardless of stale values.


Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

 Fixes https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/137886
 KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/2570

 #### Special notes for your reviewer:

title: Remove reconcilePodMemoryProtection that resets pod cgroup values on systemd
content:
#### Special notes for your reviewer:
The root cause is that systemd's dbus call resets properties not included in the call to their defaults. When we only pass , systemd resets (and CPU properties) to infinity/defaults. This is a known systemd behavior as properties are set as a group, not individually.

content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug

 

What this PR does / why we need it:

title: DRA: fix Pods in a scheduling gang sharing ResourceClaims
content: When gang-scheduled Pods share ResourceClaims, subsequent Pods in the gang after the first are blocked by the DynamicResources plugin in PreFilter due to the claim's allocation still being pending. The claim is expected to be pending in PreFilter for gang scheduling, so the scheduler should reuse that pending allocation and narrow down the nodes that can accommodate those subsequent Pods according to the pending allocation like it does for completed allocations in the non-gang scheduling case.

title: kubelet: fix containers not restarting when sidecar keeps running
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind regression

 

What this PR does / why we need it:

When a pod has a sidecar (initContainer with restartPolicy: Always) with a
startupProbe, and one or more regular containers crash after a kubelet restart,
the kubelet fails to restart the regular containers. RestartCount stays at 0
indefinitely.

title: kubelet: fix containers not restarting when sidecar keeps running
content:
When ChangeContainerStatusOnKubeletRestart is disabled (the default since
v1.35), the prober worker skips seeding probe results for containers that
predate the kubelet restart. For a sidecar with a startupProbe this means
startupManager.Get() returns found=false permanently after the restart. In
computeInitContainerActions, the sidecar Running case checks the startup
manager result and breaks out early when found=false, never setting

title: kubelet: fix containers not restarting when sidecar keeps running
content:
manager result and breaks out early when found=false, never setting
podHasInitialized=true. computePodActions then returns early at the
!hasInitialized guard before it can add the crashed regular containers to
ContainersToStart.

title: kubelet: fix containers not restarting when sidecar keeps running
content:
Fix: when the gate is off and a restartable init container's startup probe is
being seeded for the first time after a kubelet restart, check the container's
Started field in the pod status. If Started=true, the sidecar had already
passed startup before the restart, so seed the startup manager with Success.
Readiness and liveness probes on sidecars are left unseeded, preserving the
behaviour of setReadyStateOnKubeletRestart.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

title: kubelet: fix containers not restarting when sidecar keeps running
content:
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Fixes: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136910

 
 (issue in a different repository)
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/ 

If there is no associated issue, then write "N/A".
-->

Special notes for your reviewer:

title: kubelet: fix containers not restarting when sidecar keeps running
content:
Special notes for your reviewer:

The Started field in the pod status is set by setReadyStateOnKubeletRestart and
preserved across kubelet restarts, making it a reliable signal that the sidecar
had completed startup before the restart occurred. Using it here avoids any
dependency on probe worker timing relative to the sync loop.

A dedicated CI lane that runs with --feature-gates=ChangeContainerStatusOnKubeletRestart=false
was added via test-infra PR #36524 and is passing on this PR.

title: Memleak in client-go transport.tlsCache
content:
What happened?

We're using a long-running process that connects to thousands of kubernetes cluster via client-go (not all simultaneously).
We noticed that the more kubernetes clusters we connect to and the longer the process runs, the more memory our process consumes over time.
An analysis with pprof confirmed that it looks like a memory leak in transport.tlsCache.

What did you expect to happen?

title: Memleak in client-go transport.tlsCache
content:
What did you expect to happen?

Memory to be freed of clients that aren't used anymore, especially when the client object is not referenced anymore.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Connect to different k8s clusters with cert-base auth in a long-running process and watch memory usage grow.

Anything else we need to know?

title: Memleak in client-go transport.tlsCache
content:
Reason for the leak:
We're using which eventually calls 
That transport calls : https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/3b5044fefd5a9a192b379236fac11ac8c41a7017/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/transport/transport.go#L50

 is a non-exported global that implements a http transport cache. The also stores the given transport in the cache if it's not there, yet.

title: Memleak in client-go transport.tlsCache
content:
However there are no mechanisms that would remove something from the cache. Neither explicitly, nor through expiration or anything.
The cache key includes k8s cert data which means every cluster we connect to will create a new cache entry that will then stay there indefinitely.

title: Fix FSWatcher goroutine leak by adding ctx to Run()
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

Reopen of https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135078. Addressed feedback https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135078#issuecomment-3484884454 from @carlory

title: Fix FSWatcher goroutine leak by adding ctx to Run()
content: FSWatcher.Run() spawned a goroutine with no exit mechanism, causing a goroutine leak. Add a ctx context.Context parameter to Run() so the goroutine can exit cleanly when the context is canceled, and defer-close the underlying fsnotify watcher on exit.

title: Fix FSWatcher goroutine leak by adding ctx to Run()
content:
For kube-proxy, the existing ctx from runLoop() is passed directly. For the flexvolume prober, ctx is stored in flexVolumeProber at construction time via GetDynamicPluginProber(), representing the component lifetime (kubelet/controller-manager), which is the appropriate scope for this long-running watcher.

afaik these are internal k8s pkgs and should not have any compatibility concerns with changing function signature.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

title: Fix FSWatcher goroutine leak by adding ctx to Run()
content:

The methods in and have a logic bug: when succeeds ( ), the condition evaluates to , so the method returns immediately — skipping the poll loop that waits for the object to actually be gone from the API server.

This means the between-subtest discovery reset poll ( ) has to absorb both the object deletion latency and discovery convergence time, causing flakes on slow CI nodes.

title: kubernetes is reconfiguring iptables/netfilter data structures with bad settings
content:
What happened?

Hello,

I've been debugging issues with my Kubernetes nodes having very high CPU usage in network components.

I've finally traced down the origin to that block of code, which is overriding sysctl settings for iptables with insanely high values.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/proxy/conntrack/sysctls.go#L62

title: kubernetes is reconfiguring iptables/netfilter data structures with bad settings
content: The default is for the kernel to configurable iptables data structures based on available memory. Typically 32k-256k buckets and 256k max entries (this may be a tad low for the most busy servers).

title: kubernetes is reconfiguring iptables/netfilter data structures with bad settings
content:
Problem: Kubernetes is reconfiguring the iptables data structures based on the number of CPU cores. It's broken on machines with many CPU.
On my servers with 512 cores, I am seeing Kubernetes reconfigure the kernel to have 16M iptables entries with a 4M hash table.
That's a 32 MB hash table that can't fit in CPU cache, with 6 GB of entries. It's ridiculous.

title: kubernetes is reconfiguring iptables/netfilter data structures with bad settings
content: The large data structure is causing constant thrashing of the CPU cache on all cores, as every network packet has to lookup the table multiple times, and network packets are distributed across all CPU cores for processing.

title: kubernetes is reconfiguring iptables/netfilter data structures with bad settings
content:
Some operations need to go over the whole hashtable, for read or write (write has locks), which is affected by the large size of the table. This is why you've been having issues with nginx proxy (among other bugs), you've got bug tickets investigating performance issue with processing/updating that table, but nobody pointing out that it should not have millions of items, no wonder it's slow to process!

Can you please correct this code?

title: kubernetes is reconfiguring iptables/netfilter data structures with bad settings
content:
Can you please correct this code?

This should be set to a fixed value. I think 256k buckets, 1M max entries is a good default value that should work for >99% of users.
If anyone reaches that limit, they should check with what is consuming active entries, then they probably need to reduce the sysctl timeout to evict these entries faster, as the default is keeping them for too long.

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Regards.

What did you expect to happen?

iptables should have reasonable configuration

title: kubernetes is reconfiguring iptables/netfilter data structures with bad settings
content:
iptables should have reasonable configuration

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

you can look at sysctl settings on any kubernetes nodes with many cores and see the problem

Anything else we need to know?

No response

Kubernetes version

all versions affected

 

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

 

Cloud provider

 
on premise
 

OS version

all Linux OS are affected as kubernetes is incorrected overwriting iptables settings from the OS.

title: DRA: return error on filter timeout instead of unschedulable
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
/kind flake

What this PR does / why we need it:
I was looking at the flaky test "retries pod scheduling after updating device class" and trying to understand why the pod sometimes never recovers after the DeviceClass is restored.

title: DRA: return error on filter timeout instead of unschedulable
content: I originally thought the issue was in the queueing hints, that DeviceClass update events were getting consumed during in flight scheduling cycles and not triggering a retry. But @pohly's review made me realize that mechanism works as designed, and I might be looking at the wrong layer.

title: DRA: return error on filter timeout instead of unschedulable
content: I dug deeper into the scheduling queue code in to understand how unschedulable pods get retried. When a pod fails scheduling, what happens next depends on how it failed. If the failure is an , the pod goes to the backoff queue and retries automatically. If it's , the pod sits in the unschedulable queue waiting for a cluster event to move it out, and if that event never comes, it's stuck for up to 5 minutes.

title: DRA: return error on filter timeout instead of unschedulable
content:
The DRA filter returns when allocation times out. But a timeout is transient, it means the allocator didn't finish in time, not that the node is incompatible. So the pod ends up in the wrong queue.

This PR changes the timeout path to return instead, so the pod lands in the backoff queue and retries automatically. It also fixes a secondary issue: when the DRA filter returns on one node, was discarding successful results from other nodes. Now it keeps feasible nodes and proceeds with them.

title: DRA: return error on filter timeout instead of unschedulable
content:
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

/fix #133531

Special notes for your reviewer:

I tried to reproduced the flake locally on a cluster by reducing the DRA filter timeout to force the timeout code path. Without this fix, the test sometimes fails. With the fix, it passes.

title: Fix zero PSI metrics emitted when OS doesn't enable PSI
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

When the feature gate is enabled but the OS does not support PSI (either the kernel lacks or was booted with ), the kubelet could register with cAdvisor. This causes zero-valued PSI metrics to be emitted, which is confusing for monitoring and alerting.

title: Fix zero PSI metrics emitted when OS doesn't enable PSI
content:
The previous implementation checked and parsed for . This was unreliable because can exist even when per-cgroup PSI files — which cAdvisor actually reads — are absent ( on cgroup v1 systems).

This PR simplifies to open — the same type of per-cgroup file that cAdvisor reads PSI values from. This single check is sufficient because:
- PSI is a single kernel feature ( / boot param ) so checking determines support for all three resources (cpu, memory, io).

title: Fix zero PSI metrics emitted when OS doesn't enable PSI
content:
- On cgroup v1 systems (where cAdvisor cannot read per-cgroup PSI anyway), this file does not exist, so we correctly skip PSI metrics.
- The parsing is no longer needed since the cgroup file's absence already covers the case.

Note:
PSI is a single kernel feature ( / boot param ) that exposes atomically — checking onlys sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.pressure is sufficient to determine support for all three resource types.

The check is used in both and so that pressure metrics

title: Fix zero PSI metrics emitted when OS doesn't enable PSI
content:
The check is used in both and so that pressure metrics
are only collected when PSI is actually supported by the OS.

title: Fix pod level limit defaulting
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
/kind api-change
 

What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR implements defaulting of pod-level resource limits from aggregated
container-level limits when the pod has explicitly opted into pod-level
resource management.

Pod-level limits are defaulted only when:
- pod-level resource requests are defined, and
- all containers (including init containers) explicitly define limits for
 supported overcommittable resources (CPU and memory).

title: Fix pod level limit defaulting
content:
supported overcommittable resources (CPU and memory).

This avoids defaulting pod-level limits in cases where any container is
partially unbounded, which could otherwise allow access to unknown resource
quantities up to node allocatable capacity.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
 
 (issue in a different repository)
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/ 

If there is no associated issue, then write "N/A".

title: Fix pod level limit defaulting
content:
If there is no associated issue, then write "N/A".
-->
Fixes Pod-level limits defaults not set when all containers have limits set and pod-level request is set https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136120

Special notes for your reviewer:
Edge case clarification:

In this PR, "all containers have limits set" is interpreted to mean that each
container defines limits for all supported overcommittable resources (CPU and
memory).

title: Fix pod level limit defaulting
content:
memory).

For example, consider a pod with three containers:
- Container A sets a CPU limit but does not set a memory limit
- Container B sets a memory limit but does not set a CPU limit
- Container C sets both CPU and memory limits

Although each resource dimension is covered by at least one container, no
single container defines limits for all resources. This pod is therefore
treated as not having all container limits set, and pod-level limit
defaulting is skipped.

title: Fix pod level limit defaulting
content:
defaulting is skipped.

This behavior is intentional to avoid implicitly granting pods access to
"unknown" or unbounded resources.

title: APF should handle Deletecollection verb
content:
What happened?

APF doesn't do much for Deletecollection verb, even though it's more expensive than list (list + delete one by one)
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/2b4ef19578f6bec7f4a76c82b7afcac2c228648d/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/request/width.go#L112-L126

What did you expect to happen?

Deletecollection should take similar amount of seats as list so that there are not too many deletecollection calls get processed at the same time.

title: APF should handle Deletecollection verb
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Deleting a namespace will trigger namespace controller to garbage collected resources in that namespace using deletecollection verb. If the number objects are large enough. There can be performance issues when there are too many deletecollection calls.

title: client-go/gentype: fake.go in production package disables Go linker dead code elimination (+52 MB binary bloat)
content:
Summary

The package includes in the same Go package as production code ( ). Because Go compiles all non-test files in a package together, 's import of is included in every binary that uses typed Kubernetes clients — even when no fake/test code is used.

title: client-go/gentype: fake.go in production package disables Go linker dead code elimination (+52 MB binary bloat)
content:
This transitively pulls in and , which use with non-constant arguments. When the Go linker detects is reachable, it conservatively disables dead code elimination for all exported methods on all reachable types. This causes massive binary bloat for any program that also imports packages with large method sets, like .

Impact

title: client-go/gentype: fake.go in production package disables Go linker dead code elimination (+52 MB binary bloat)
content:
Impact

Adding a blank import of to a program that uses the AWS EC2 SDK increases binary size by +52 MB (from 13 MB to 65 MB), linking 2,273 methods instead of the 7 actually needed.

This affects every consumer of typed Kubernetes clients ( ), since they all import .

Root Cause

The import chain:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

The Go linker sees is reachable and disables method pruning globally. See golang/go#25081 and kubernetes/kubernetes#132216 for background.

Reproduction

title: client-go/gentype: fake.go in production package disables Go linker dead code elimination (+52 MB binary bloat)
content:
Reproduction

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
DIR=$(mktemp -d)
trap "rm -rf $DIR" EXIT
cd "$DIR"

go mod init gentype-dce-repro

cat > baseline.go with_gentype.go testing -> protobuf -> reflect.MethodByName` chain for production builds, allowing the Go linker to properly eliminate dead code.

Related Issues

title: client-go/gentype: fake.go in production package disables Go linker dead code elimination (+52 MB binary bloat)
content:
Related Issues

- golang/go#25081 — Enable linker dead code pruning when using reflect
- golang/go#60221 — There should be a way to determine why deadcode elimination was not performed
- kubernetes/kubernetes#132216 — Allow linker to perform dead code elimination for kube-apiserver etc
- kubernetes/kubernetes#127888 — Make client-go lighter and easier to consume (Umbrella)

/kind bug
/sig api-machinery

title: PodReadyToStartContainers condition gets flipped  to true after image pull.

What happened?

According to documentation https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/pod-lifecycle/#pod-has-network

The PodReadyToStartContainers condition is set to True by the kubelet after the successful completion of sandbox creation and network configuration for the Pod by the runtime plugin. The kubelet can start pulling container images and create containers after PodReadyToStartContainers condition has been set to True.

title: kube-proxy fails to sync in nftables mode when newer nftables sets exist
content:
What happened?

kube-proxy crashes repeatedly, causing the container network to not function.
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

What did you expect to happen?

kube-proxy should correctly setup nftables rules.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Run a container in the host namespace with a newer version of nftables (1.1.3+), for example Fedora 43. Add a nft set in the container:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: kube-proxy fails to sync in nftables mode when newer nftables sets exist
content:
Then delete the kube-proxy pod on the host, and example the logs on the new pod.

Anything else we need to know?

kube-proxy in nftables mode crashes repeatedly on hosts where newer versions of nftables (eg 1.1.3) have created sets in the same network namespace.

I found a fix for the crash in newer nftables here (it appears to be related to udata being present on newer nft sets):
https://git.netfilter.org/nftables/commit/?id=be737a1986bfee0ddea4bee7863dca0123a2bcbc

title: kube-proxy fails to sync in nftables mode when newer nftables sets exist
content:
It's probably a good idea to build kube-proxy with a patched version of nftables to avoid the crash, but not use anything newer than nftables 1.1.1 as it could then potentially break other unpatched nftables in CNIs etc.

title: Container fail to create when env var contains $ followed by non-ASCII character (invalid UTF-8 gRPC error)
content:
What happened?

After upgrading our Kubernetes cluster from v1.33.5 to v1.34.2 (AWS EKS), pods scheduled on nodes running v1.34.2 fail during container creation with the following error:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

The pods are created successfully, but containers fail to create.

title: Container fail to create when env var contains $ followed by non-ASCII character (invalid UTF-8 gRPC error)
content:
The pods are created successfully, but containers fail to create.

 

Important observation:
Pods scheduled on nodes still running v1.33.5 continue to work correctly with the same manifests and environment variables. The failure occurs only on nodes upgraded to v1.34.2, strongly indicating a regression.

Through isolation and testing, we observed that the failure occurs when an environment variable value contains a $ immediately followed by a non-ASCII character.

title: Container fail to create when env var contains $ followed by non-ASCII character (invalid UTF-8 gRPC error)
content:
This behaviour did not occur on v1.33.5 and appears to be a regression introduced in v1.34.x.

What did you expect to happen?

Pods and containers should start successfully when environment variable values contain valid UTF-8 characters, including non-ASCII characters and literal $ characters.

This configuration worked as expected on v1.33.5 nodes, even within the same cluster during a rolling upgrade.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

title: Container fail to create when env var contains $ followed by non-ASCII character (invalid UTF-8 gRPC error)
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create a pod (or deployment) with an environment variable containing:
 - a $
 - immediately followed by a non-ASCII UTF-8 character
3. Schedule the pod on a v1.34.2 node
4. Observe container creation failure with:
 - 

Minimal reproducible example:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Observed result:

❌ Fails on v1.34.2 nodes

✅ Works on v1.33.5 nodes

Expected result:
Container starts normally on all versions.

title: Container fail to create when env var contains $ followed by non-ASCII character (invalid UTF-8 gRPC error)
content:
Expected result:
Container starts normally on all versions.

Anything else we need to know?

- We run a custom CRD/operator that propagates env vars from the CR spec into Deployments and multi-container Pods.
- The failure occurs after Pod creation, during container creation / runtime interaction.
- This reproduces across all containers in the pod that receive the env var.
- This is reproducible in a mixed-version cluster:
 - Pods on v1.33.5 nodes work
 - Pods on v1.34.2 nodes fail

title: WIP - protect against race between deletion and adding finalizers
content:
Reattempt of https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129768, which was reverted in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/133979

The first merge started flaking e2e tests on "not found" errors deleting pods. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/133976

I found and addressed two gaps in the original PR in subsequent commits with tests:

title: WIP - protect against race between deletion and adding finalizers
content: * When a graceful deletion object is both updated and then immediately deleted in the store#Delete function, if something else deleted the object in between, Delete tolerates a NotFound error and returns the last updated object. If the Delete now fails on an RV precondition error, the Get we do on the reattempt should also react to a NotFound error in the same way to preserve user-facing behavior.

title: WIP - protect against race between deletion and adding finalizers
content:
* When a graceful deletion object is both updated and deleted in the store#Delete function, the RV from the updated object must be used as the internal RV precondition.

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

/sig api-machinery

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug


What this PR does / why we need it:
On WSL2 with Docker Desktop, certain mount entries in /proc/mounts contain Windows paths with unescaped spaces in the mount options field (e.g., 'path=C:\Program Files\Docker\Docker\resources').

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
Key Point: This issue only occurs when WSL2 with Docker Desktop is used as a Kubernetes worker node joining a cluster. Running containers directly in WSL2 works fine - the issue is specific to kubelet's mounts parsing during node initialization.

When parsed by strings.Fields(), these lines produce 7+ fields instead of the expected 6, causing kubelet to fail with:
 'Failed to start ContainerManager: system validation failed -
 wrong number of fields (expected 6, got 7)'

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
wrong number of fields (expected 6, got 7)'

This change modifies parseProcMounts() to:
- Accept lines with 6 or more fields (instead of exactly 6)
- Parse dump (fsfreq) and pass (fspassno) from the end of the line
- Join any extra middle fields into the options string

This maintains backward compatibility with standard 6-field entries while properly handling edge cases with spaces in mount options.


Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes #135554

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes #135554
 
 (issue in a different repository)
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/ 

If there is no associated issue, then write "N/A".
-->

Special notes for your reviewer:
- The fix parses dump (fsfreq) and pass (fspassno) from the end of the line since they are always single integers (0, 1, or 2)
- Standard 6-field /proc/mounts entries are parsed identically to before

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
- Standard 6-field /proc/mounts entries are parsed identically to before
- Added test case for WSL mount entry with 7 fields
- All existing mount-utils tests pass

What did you expect to happen?

Kubelet should start successfully on WSL2 with Docker Desktop installed. The /proc/mounts parser should handle mount entries where the options field contains unescaped spaces.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Install Windows with WSL2 and Docker Desktop for Windows with WSL2 backend enabled
2. Inside WSL2 Ubuntu, install Kubernetes components (kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl v1.34.2)
3. Join the WSL2 node to an existing Kubernetes cluster running on a separate Linux control-plane using kubeadm join

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
4. Observe kubelet fails to start with error: "Failed to start ContainerManager: system validation failed - wrong number of fields (expected 6, got 7)"

Root Cause:

Docker Desktop mounts Windows paths in WSL2's mounts with unescaped spaces in the options field

Example problematic entry:
C:\134Program\040Files\134Docker\134Docker\134resources /Docker/host 9p rw,noatime,aname=drvfs;path=C:\Program Files\Docker\Docker\resources;symlinkroot=/mnt/,...

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
The options field contains path=C:\Program Files\Docker\Docker\resources with unescaped spaces
When strings.Fields() parses this line, it splits on whitespace, creating 7-8 fields instead of the expected 6

To verify the problematic mount entry:
$ cat /proc/mounts | grep "Program Files"
 Check field count (should be 7-8 instead of 6)
$ cat /proc/mounts | grep "Program Files" | awk '{print NF}'

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
$ cat /proc/mounts | grep "Program Files" | awk '{print NF}'

Key Point: This issue only occurs when WSL2 with Docker Desktop is used as a Kubernetes worker node joining a cluster. Running containers directly in WSL2 works fine - the issue is specific to kubelet's mounts parsing during node initialization.

Anything else we need to know?

title: mount-utils: Handle /proc/mounts with >6 fields
content:
Anything else we need to know?

This affects WSL2 users who have Docker Desktop installed. The /proc/mounts format defines 6 fields (device, mountpoint, type, options, dump, pass), but the options field can contain unescaped spaces in 9p/drvfs filesystems used by WSL.

Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
Yes - Kubelet now starts successfully on WSL2 systems with Docker Desktop installed.

title: PV Controller Serial Processing Bottleneck Causes Massive Pod Scheduling Delays in Large-Scale StatefulSet Scenarios
content: What happened?

title: PV Controller Serial Processing Bottleneck Causes Massive Pod Scheduling Delays in Large-Scale StatefulSet Scenarios
content: In large-scale StatefulSet scenarios where each pod is bound to a PVC using volumeTemplate, we observe that a large number of pods remain in the Pending state. The root cause is that the PVCs of these pods are not bound to PVs. The fundamental issue is that the PV controller processes PVCs serially

title: PV Controller Serial Processing Bottleneck Causes Massive Pod Scheduling Delays in Large-Scale StatefulSet Scenarios
content: . The fundamental issue is that the PV controller processes PVCs serially. Each PVC requires at least two rounds of processing by the PV controller to reach the Bound state: the first round triggers PV creation, and the second round triggers the binding of the PV to the PVC. Moreover, after a PV is created, it is stored in the PV controller's cache (persistentVolumeOrderedIndex) only after the PV controller processes the PV

title: PV Controller Serial Processing Bottleneck Causes Massive Pod Scheduling Delays in Large-Scale StatefulSet Scenarios
content: . Then, during the second round of processing the PVC, the PV controller can find the PV from the cache (via findBestMatchForClaim) and bind them. This multiple-step processing can take a significant amount of time. In our tests with 50,000 PVCs, it took up to 20 minutes for a pod to be scheduled (the scheduler waits for the PVC to be bound).

title: PV Controller Serial Processing Bottleneck Causes Massive Pod Scheduling Delays in Large-Scale StatefulSet Scenarios
content:
What did you expect to happen?

We expect that pods are scheduled quickly, and PVCs are bound to PVs without significant delays, even in large-scale scenarios.

In dynamic provisioning scenarios with WaitForFirstConsumer binding mode, the relationship between PVC and PV is deterministic. Therefore, we propose making the volumeWorker and claimWorker in the PV controller concurrent. This would significantly improve processing efficiency without causing data consistency issues.

title: PV Controller Serial Processing Bottleneck Causes Massive Pod Scheduling Delays in Large-Scale StatefulSet Scenarios
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create a StatefulSet with a large number of replicas (e.g., 50,000)
2. Use volumeTemplate to create a PVC for each pod
3. Use dynamic provisioning with the binding mode set to WaitForFirstConsumer
4. Observe the pod scheduling status and the time taken for PVCs to bind

Anything else we need to know?

title: PV Controller Serial Processing Bottleneck Causes Massive Pod Scheduling Delays in Large-Scale StatefulSet Scenarios
content:
Anything else we need to know?

In dynamic provisioning scenarios with WaitForFirstConsumer binding mode, the relationship between PVC and PV is deterministic. Therefore, we propose making the volumeWorker and claimWorker in the PV controller concurrent. This would significantly improve processing efficiency without causing data consistency issues.

title: On-demand per-pod PLEG Relist
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

Most container runtime operations are synchronous, meaning we can expect to see the results of the call as soon as it returns. This means that if the Kubelet takes an action in SyncPod, we can relist the pod immediately to see the updated results, rather than waiting for the next global relist (every 2s).

title: On-demand per-pod PLEG Relist
content:
Here are the benchmark results (run locally on kind) from this change (averaged over 1000 runs, running 10 operations in parallel)

Create pod: 1.798s → 1.136s
Resize pod: 1.778s → 0.164s (10x improvement!)

title: client-go: fix goroutine hot-loop in StartEventWatcher on broadcaster shutdown
content:
/kind bug
/sig api-machinery

What this PR does / why we need it:

 launches a goroutine that reads events from a channel:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

When is called, the following sequence occurs:

1. blocks until closes every watcher's result channel.
2. is called to signal the cancellation context.

title: client-go: fix goroutine hot-loop in StartEventWatcher on broadcaster shutdown
content:
Between steps 1 and 2 -- and also in configurations where no cancellation context exists -- the closed channel returns the zero-value on every iteration without blocking. The goroutine enters a hot loop that burns CPU until the context is cancelled or the process exits.

This PR guards against the closed-channel condition using the idiomatic two-value receive:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: client-go: fix goroutine hot-loop in StartEventWatcher on broadcaster shutdown
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

A regression test, , is added. It calls with no cancellation context and asserts via that no goroutines are leaked. This test fails on the un-patched code and passes after the fix.

On event safety

 is fully synchronous: it drains the channel completely before invoking . All in-flight events are delivered to every watcher handler before the result channel is closed. Returning when therefore incurs zero event loss.

Why this is a real issue (re: @sttts)

title: client-go: fix goroutine hot-loop in StartEventWatcher on broadcaster shutdown
content:
Why this is a real issue (re: @sttts)

This is not a theoretical concern. The window between closing channels and firing is OS-scheduler dependent and can be arbitrarily long under load. The -backed regression test reproduces the goroutine leak deterministically.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

- is already installed in , so goroutine leak detection is package-wide.

title: Fix RepairIPAddress controller startup failure when namespace informer is not yet synced
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
 

What this PR does / why we need it:

During apiserver startup on clusters upgrading from v1.32 to v1.33 with enabled, the IP address repair controller ( ) runs in a PostStartHook and attempts to create objects for existing services. On clusters with many namespaces (330k+), the namespace informer takes a long time to sync. During this window, the admission plugin webhook rejects requests with errors because it considers itself "not yet ready".

title: Fix RepairIPAddress controller startup failure when namespace informer is not yet synced
content:
Previously, only retried on errors. This PR changes it to also retry on errors, allowing the repair controller to succeed once the namespace informer finishes syncing, instead of failing the PostStartHook and preventing the apiserver from becoming ready.

Changes:
- In , switch from to with a custom predicate that retries on both and errors
- Add an integration test that reproduces the issue with 330k namespaces (skipped in short mode due to ~30 min runtime)

title: scheduler: optimize podGroupInfo to minimize the lock time
content:
Gang scheduler will add the pod into the podGroupInfo before pod enqueue, if there are thousands of pods in a podGroupInfo, The call of AssumedPods, AssignedPods and AllPods will hold the lock and clone the map, so that new Pods should wait there for a long time to add into the podGroupInfo, also it will be a long wait to enqueue the pod.

In our test, a pod will wait seconds to enqueue if we have 5000 pod in a gang group.

title: scheduler: optimize podGroupInfo to minimize the lock time
content:
In this PR, we can avoid the traverse and clone of the map by adding AllPodsCount and ScheduledPodsCount method. Actually in most of the time, what the scheduler needs to know is the count of them but not the contents.

We also combined the Assumed and Assigned pods into a set named "scheduledPods" so we can quickly get the count of them, which is also scheduler really needs to get.

 

What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind api-change


What this PR does / why we need it:

title: scheduler: optimize podGroupInfo to minimize the lock time
content:
/kind bug
/kind api-change


What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR can optimize the gang scheduler efficiency, in our test, the schedule time of 5000 pods per gang is reduced from more than 30min to less than 3min.

title: PV controller is slow when there are many released PVs
content: What happened?

title: PV controller is slow when there are many released PVs
content:
We have seen PVC binding (no dynamic provisioning) to take minutes when there are thousands of released PVs in the cluster. This is common when using local PVs in the cloud, where nodes get deleted more frequently and leave behind stale local PV objects.

It looks like PV controller is getting throttled by client-go, but the API server does not seem loaded, and there does not seem to be a signficant amount of UPDATEs from the PV controller (10 in a few minutes).
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: PV controller is slow when there are many released PVs
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

From code inspection, here's what I've found so far:

Potentially unnecessary Get() calls: 
- https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/v1.27.0-alpha.2/pkg/controller/volume/persistentvolume/pv_controller.go#L643
- https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/fb57080e069fe2efe11734a4570a92873e673c35/pkg/controller/volume/persistentvolume/pv_controller.go#L1295

Unbind volume does not check if updated is needed:

title: PV controller is slow when there are many released PVs

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/fb57080e069fe2efe11734a4570a92873e673c35/pkg/controller/volume/persistentvolume/pv_controller.go#L1110

It looks like client-go throttling is done per rest client, so a lot of GETs could cause UPDATEs to get throttled:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/fb57080e069fe2efe11734a4570a92873e673c35/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/request.go#L624

/kind bug

 

What this PR does / why we need it:

If /var/lib/kubelet is MS_SHARED mountpoint, all the mountpoints under /var/lib/kubelet will have duplicate one. When is executed, it will try to umount one path twice. However, they are in the peer group. Once we umount one path, the duplicate one will be umounted as well. So, in this case, we should ignore EINVAL error.

title: Pod status redundant relist
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug
/kind cleanup

What this PR does / why we need it:

Today, the generic PLEG works like this:

1. Get all pods ( )
 1. Get all sandboxes
 2. Get all containers
 3. group containers & sandboxes into pods ( )
2. Compare this new state to the previous state. If there are changes:
3. For each pod that changed, get an updated status ( )
 1. Get the pod sandboxes
 2. Get the pod containers
 3. For each sandbox, get its status

title: Pod status redundant relist
content:
2. Get the pod containers
 3. For each sandbox, get its status
 4. For each container, get its status
 5. Combine into object

Steps 3.1 and 3.2 are redundant with 1.1 and 1.2.

This PR:
1. Refactors GetPodStatus to take a (from step 1.3), so it can skip the repeated runtime calls.
2. Adds a new function that is the singular equivalent of . Also includes some refactoring within kuberuntime to avoid code duplication for the new GetPod function.

A security issue was discovered in ingress-nginx where the nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target Ingress annotation can be used to inject configuration into nginx. title: Include relevant dimensions in pod controller indexing
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

The pod controller uid index currently only includes the uid of the owner reference.

The namespace, kind, and name of the owner must also match for the owner reference to be considered a match.

This PR includes those dimensions in the index, unifies the key building between orphaned and non-orphaned pods, and makes all accesses go through the key builder to ensure there is no direct use of OrphanPodIndexKey.

title: Include relevant dimensions in pod controller indexing
content:
This should be picked to release branches that introduced controller uid indexing (1.33 and 1.34) to resolve possible controller confusion in the presence of incorrect ownerReference uids.

See https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/92743 for similar issues the garbage collection controller hit when indexing only on uid.

title: Volume expansion e2e tests fail when CSI driver allocates larger volumes than requested

The test should use the actual allocated volume size when calculating the new size for expansion, not the user-requested size.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

title: Volume expansion e2e tests fail when CSI driver allocates larger volumes than requested

1. Use a CSI driver that has minimum volume size constraints (e.g., Azure File nfs CSI driver with 100Gi minimum)
 2. Create a PVC requesting a size smaller than the driver's minimum (e.g., 5Gi)
 3. The driver allocates the minimum size (e.g., 100Gi) instead
 4. Run volume expansion e2e tests
 5. Tests fail because:
 - = 5Gi (user requested)
 - = 100Gi (actual allocated)
 - Test calculates: newSize = 5Gi + 1Gi = 6Gi
 - Expansion fails because 6Gi

title: Fix partial uncoreCache CPU allocation with partially available cores
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
When SMT is enabled and some cores have only one sibling thread available, takePartialUncore may select cores that don't provide enough CPUs. This happens because numCoresNeeded assumes all cores have full CPUsPerCore threads free.

title: Fix partial uncoreCache CPU allocation with partially available cores
content:
For example, with topoUncoreSingleSocketSMT and available CPUs {3,5,7,11-15}, requesting 5 CPUs calculates numCoresNeeded=3, but the first 3 cores in UncoreCache 1 only yield 4 CPUs. This causes partial uncore allocation to fail and fall back to a cross-uncoreCache path.
Fixes #137315

title: VolumeAttributesClass update should support recover from failure
content:
What happened?

- When setting a VolumeAttributesClass (VAC) name in PVC for the first time, currently there isn't a way for the user to go back to unsetting VAC name even if it keeps failing and there is no way of setting VAC name successfully on the storage backend.
- When changing a VAC name from A->B, currently there isn't a way for the user to go back to VAC A if it keeps failing and there is no way of changing from A->B successfully on the storage backend.

What did you expect to happen?

title: VolumeAttributesClass update should support recover from failure
content:
What did you expect to happen?

- When setting a VolumeAttributesClass (VAC) name in PVC for the first time, there should be a way for the user to roll back (unset the VAC name) if it keeps failing and if there is no way of setting the VAC name successfully on the storage backend.
- When changing a VAC name from A->B, there should be a way for the user to roll back (change VAC name back to A) if it keeps failing and if there is no way of changing from A->B successfully on the storage backend.

title: VolumeAttributesClass update should support recover from failure
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

- Try to set VAC name in PVC for the first time but providing a VAC that is infeasible to set on the storage backend so it will never be successful.
- Try to change a VAC name from A->B but B is an infeasible option for the storage backend so it will never be successful.

Anything else we need to know?

title: VolumeAttributesClass update should support recover from failure
content:
Anything else we need to know?

If CSI driver returns an error code that indicates CSI ModifyVolume is infeasible, we should allow the user to roll back the VAC change in PVC.

title: Handle NotFound errors in garbage collector
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

This PR fixes issue #136525 where the Garbage Collector logs spurious error messages when attempting to delete objects that have already been deleted externally.

In scenarios where users delete pods directly after deleting jobs (to free up resources faster), the GC later attempts to delete these already-deleted pods, resulting in NotFound errors being logged as errors.

How does this PR address the issue?

The fix adds explicit NotFound error handling in the function after calls. When a NotFound error occurs:

title: IPv6 support check in kube-proxy fails to detect if IPv6 support is disabled
content:
What happened?

While working on a dual-stack issue on (#114702 #137370), I discovered another issue.

If the IPv6 support is turned off on a node (for example, with sysctl), kube-proxy fails to correctly detect that the node is single‑stack, and logs the following error when a dual-service is deployed:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
If dual-stack mode is enabled incorrectly, the following error occurs:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

What did you expect to happen?

title: IPv6 support check in kube-proxy fails to detect if IPv6 support is disabled
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

What did you expect to happen?

Dual-stack support should not be enabled, and the following message should be logged instead:

 

The problem is on these lines: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/bd3c97fbd8d79741ba71f85a1eb342c01266e2b9/cmd/kube-proxy/app/server_linux.go#L107-L112 -- they do not take into account whether IPv6 support has been disabled. This option has existed since Linux kernel v2.6.27.

title: IPv6 support check in kube-proxy fails to detect if IPv6 support is disabled
content:
How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

It can happen that a claim gets deallocated in parallel to adding a new pod to
ReservedFor. Without binding conditions, that was caught by the apiserver
validation. With binding conditions, the code which checks and sets
AllocationTimestamp panics with a nil pointer access.

This has been observed in the TestDRA/all/ShareResourceClaimSequentially
