title: Taint Eviction Controller sometimes doesn't evict
content:
What happened?

Hello
we have run into the weirdest issue. Very randomly (we have 150 clusters and it happens on roughly 1/5th of them) sometimes the taint eviction controller doesn't evict nodes after them being 5 minutes as NotReady (eg during maintenance when they are shut down).

title: Taint Eviction Controller sometimes doesn't evict
content: I can see in the controller-manager's log that it evicts one NotReady node, but second one gets ignored for some reason. And only sometimes. It is not a matter of a specific pod or a deployment, but it doesn't drain some nodes -- so far I've seen it only on nodes tainted as controlplane, but I was unable to find anything in the source code that would relate to this behaviour - especially if it works on other controlplane nodes of other clusters just fine.

title: Taint Eviction Controller sometimes doesn't evict
content:
We have been unable to figure out any correlation between k8s versions (mix of 1.32.1 and 1.32.2), random pod statuses etc. There's nothing in the logs either and I have nothing to go by, it is completely random and restarting controller-manager does NOT fix this! There must be some hidden logic that I am overlooking.

All clusters are set up the same way, using kubeadm, usually upgraded from some older releases (that's the only difference between them).

title: Taint Eviction Controller sometimes doesn't evict
content:
I am more looking at possible idea what I should look at further diagnosing this, because I am losing my mind :(

What did you expect to happen?

Drain nodes after 5 minutes of them being NotReady

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Hard to say, but it seems like something in the order of "setting up 1.28 cluster using kubeadm, upgrading to 1.32.x branch and hard shutting it down" but I am not 100% sure it will always trigger.

Anything else we need to know?

content:
Anything else we need to know?

title: Rework container lifecycle tests to not require "privileged" pod security
content: See discussion in #133091. The Container Lifecycle Hook e2e tests were designed to use the field of the probes, which is now banned by PSA. This reworks them so that instead of having the probe target a different pod directly, the probes now target a sidecar container in the same pod as the container being tested, and that sidecar container pokes the other pod

title: Ensure latest resource state is fetched consistently in `kubectl describe`
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR unifies the resource retrieval logic in by adding the call to the resource builder.

Currently, there is an inconsistency in how fetches data depending on the input source:
- When running , the resource is retrieved directly from the server.
- When running , the command relies on the local file content rather than fetching the live state from the cluster.

title: Ensure latest resource state is fetched consistently in `kubectl describe`
content:
By adding to the builder chain, we ensure that even when a resource is initialized from a local file, will perform a request to the API server to retrieve the most up-to-date version of that object before describing it. This ensures that the output of describe always reflects the actual state of the cluster.

The included test case verifies that an API call is made when describing a resource provided via a file path.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

title: cmd/kubeadm: ignore EINVAL error during unmount
content:
What type of PR is this?

/kind bug

 

What this PR does / why we need it:

If /var/lib/kubelet is MS_SHARED mountpoint, all the mountpoints under /var/lib/kubelet will have duplicate one. When is executed, it will try to umount one path twice. However, they are in the peer group. Once we umount one path, the duplicate one will be umounted as well. So, in this case, we should ignore EINVAL error.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

title: CVE-2024-3744: azure-file-csi-driver discloses service account tokens in logs
content:
CVSS Rating: CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N - MEDIUM (6.5)

A security issue was discovered in azure-file-csi-driver where an actor with access to the driver logs could observe service account tokens. These tokens could then potentially be exchanged with external cloud providers to access secrets stored in cloud vault solutions. Tokens are only logged when TokenRequests is configured in the CSIDriver object and the driver is set to run at log level 2 or greater via the -v flag.

title: CVE-2024-3744: azure-file-csi-driver discloses service account tokens in logs
content:
This issue has been rated MEDIUM CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N (6.5), and assigned CVE-2024-3744

Am I vulnerable?

You may be vulnerable if TokenRequests is configured in the CSIDriver object and the driver is set to run at log level 2 or greater via the -v flag.

To check if token requests are configured, run the following command:

kubectl get csidriver file.csi.azure.com -o jsonpath="{.spec.tokenRequests}"

title: CVE-2024-3744: azure-file-csi-driver discloses service account tokens in logs
content:
To check if tokens are being logged, examine the secrets-store container log:

kubectl logs csi-azurefile-controller-56bfddd689-dh5tk -c azurefile -f | grep --line-buffered "csi.storage.k8s.io/serviceAccount.tokens"

Affected Versions

- azure-file-csi-driver <= v1.29.3
- azure-file-csi-driver v1.30.0

How do I mitigate this vulnerability?

Prior to upgrading, this vulnerability can be mitigated by running azure-file-csi-driver at log level 0 or 1 via the -v flag.

title: CVE-2024-3744: azure-file-csi-driver discloses service account tokens in logs
content:
Fixed Versions

- azure-file-csi-driver v1.29.4
- azure-file-csi-driver v1.30.1

To upgrade, refer to the documentation: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/azurefile-csi-driver?tab=readme-ov-file#install-driver-on-a-kubernetes-cluster 

Detection

Examine cloud provider logs for unexpected token exchanges, as well as unexpected access to cloud resources.

If you find evidence that this vulnerability has been exploited, please contact security@kubernetes.io

Acknowledgements

title: Use `secrets` field in `NodePublishVolumeRequest` for sa tokens
content: If the field is set in the spec, the tokens are generated and funneled from kubelet to driver as part of volume attributes map. The key for the tokens in the map is . The service account tokens can be used for workload identity in cloud providers. Especially in case of Secrets Store CSI Driver, these service account tokens are used to access external secrets store to get secrets and mount in the volume.

title: Use `secrets` field in `NodePublishVolumeRequest` for sa tokens
content: As these tokens are sensitive information, it doesn't feel right to include these in the volume context map along with pod name, namespace, service account name which are non-sensitive information. There is a dedicated secrets field in the proto which today is being used for that would be more appropriate for tokens.

title: Use `secrets` field in `NodePublishVolumeRequest` for sa tokens
content: To provide more context on "why this issue", we had a CVE in the Secrets Store CSI Driver (ref: CVE-2023-2878) where the service account tokens were logged as part of the GRPC request in the driver. We use the protosanitizer in the driver to sanitize the requests before logging but given volume context is not really considered a "secret", the tokens were skipped and logged

title: Use `secrets` field in `NodePublishVolumeRequest` for sa tokens
content: . We got around this by building custom logic for logging in the driver to strip the tokens but this is something every driver would need to do to be careful and not log the tokens. A change could be made in protosanitizer to sanitize this but given volume context is a map, either entire map needs to be marked a secret or sanitizing has to be done on a field by field basis

title: Use `secrets` field in `NodePublishVolumeRequest` for sa tokens
content: . Although this prompted me to create this issue, I think volume context is not really "secrets" and appropriate field for sensitive sa tokens would be the secrets field.

title: Use `secrets` field in `NodePublishVolumeRequest` for sa tokens

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
/kind regression
/kind failing-test

What this PR does / why we need it:

PR #135196 promoted from Alpha to Beta (default: true),
causing DRA health monitoring e2e tests to run for the first time in the
 CI job. These tests fail intermittently due to
a pre-existing kubelet race in the cgroup-per-qos kill path.

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content:
Root cause: During PLEG-triggered , the cgroup-per-qos code path at
 checks . On cgroup v2 with
the systemd cgroup driver (containerd 1.7), can return false
after has already been called — systemd may delete cgroup
subsystems (particularly cpuset) that libcontainer created via ,
causing the validation to fail when it checks all whitelisted
controller paths. This triggers with a 30s grace period, blocking
the pod worker and preventing DRA health updates from propagating to the pod

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content: status. The test's 30s timeout races with the kill's 30s grace period.

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content:
On containerd 2.0 (also systemd cgroup driver), this race is not observed,
likely due to differences in how the runtime interacts with cgroup subsystem
creation.

Fix (three commits):

1. Test-level fix ( ): Wait for to
 appear in the pod status (indicating the DRA health pipeline is active) before
 sending health updates. Also increase the verification timeout from 30s to 60s.

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content:
2. Kubelet-level fix ( ): Track pods for which has
 been called during the current kubelet process lifetime. Skip the cgroup kill
 path for these pods, since returning false after 
 indicates a cgroup validation issue rather than a need for cgroup migration.
 The tracking set is empty on kubelet startup, preserving the original
 kubelet-restart cgroup migration behavior.

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content:
3. Unit test ( ): Add 
 that directly reproduces the race condition by configuring 
 to return and verifying that SyncPod kills the pod without the
 fix but skips the kill when was called.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Regression introduced by #135196
Failing CI job: https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/ci-node-e2e-containerd-1-7-dra/2029019384965500928

Special notes for your reviewer:

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content:
Special notes for your reviewer:

The three commits are intentionally separate and could be split into individual PRs
if preferred:
- Commit 1 (test fix) unblocks CI immediately with no kubelet behavior change.
- Commit 2 (kubelet fix) addresses the root cause.
- Commit 3 (unit test) reproduces the race condition and verifies the fix.

title: kubelet: fix cgroup-per-qos kill race for DRA health monitoring on containerd 1.7
content:
The kubelet fix preserves the key invariant: on kubelet restart, the
 set is empty, so the cgroup kill path still triggers for
containers from a previous kubelet lifetime that need cgroup migration. The fix
only suppresses the kill for pods whose cgroups were already initialized in the
current process lifetime.

Cleanup of the tracking set happens in both and
 (after ) to prevent memory leaks across all
pod lifecycle paths (removal, eviction, termination).

title: statefulset: wrap errors with %w in StatefulPodControl
content:
/kind bug

What this PR does / why we need it:

Some fmt.Errorf calls in StatefulPodControl used %s when formatting
errors returned from API calls.

Using %s converts the error to plain text and breaks the error chain,
preventing callers from using errors.Is / errors.As to detect specific
API errors (e.g. NotFound or Conflict).

This PR replaces %s with %w where appropriate to preserve the original
error. Additionally, one error path in ClaimsMatchRetentionPolicy

```release-note
title: If node reports falsely Ready before it creates server CSR, certificate templates never populate and server CSR is not created
content:
What happened?

When debugging https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/130001 with @lentzi90, we tried so many ways to figure out why the CSR was not created. One was #130409 and another one was that if node is considering itself while not actually being Ready, before it creates server CSR, those will be forever stuck as it does not get templates. Client CSR and cert are provisioned properly.

title: If node reports falsely Ready before it creates server CSR, certificate templates never populate and server CSR is not created
content:
Detecting this loop required additional logging to be added, such as @lentzi90's branch here for 1.32 or here for main.

Logs:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
and it sits there for all eternity, printing it does not have certificate template.

What did you expect to happen?

Kubelet to initialize itself, request client and server CSRs as configured with bootstrapping and rotation in kubeadm config.

How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

title: If node reports falsely Ready before it creates server CSR, certificate templates never populate and server CSR is not created
content:
Now this is tricky part. Kubelet thinks its ready when it has CNI config files in and it has some routes (not exactly sure what counts). This means this issue can be occuring only when resetting an existing node, or maybe when upgrading, or some other lifecycle management operations are run, or maybe if someone optimizes the node installation somehow. A clean VM and and first time kubeadm init will not run into this issue AFAIK.

Using very simple kubeadm config:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: If node reports falsely Ready before it creates server CSR, certificate templates never populate and server CSR is not created
content:
Using very simple kubeadm config:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

we init the cluster, and then install Calico on it (to get the config, and the routes):

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
and we can see node turns way before Calico manages to run more than one or two calico-node containers (which create the configs/binaries/routes). We then do some LCM operation, simulated here with a reset, without manually nuking more than kubeadm is cleaning up:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: If node reports falsely Ready before it creates server CSR, certificate templates never populate and server CSR is not created
content: If we now run again, it will report node Ready (as it has everything in place right away, without installing Calico again) and CSR will be created just fine. But there are certain conditions where node thinks it is still Ready, but it fails, especially related to networking/routes, which sometimes happen during maintenance and are later really hard to catch and debug, as Kubelet does not print a single thing related to server CSRs, unless instrumented as documented above.

title: If node reports falsely Ready before it creates server CSR, certificate templates never populate and server CSR is not created
content:
Would a PR that adds some logging to server CSR creation, say at level 4, be acceptable?

Anything else we need to know?

I apologize for the lack of exact reproduction steps. I managed to achieve this condition several times during CSR creation debugging, but due nature of debugging failed to capture/understand the exact condition that satisfies the node checks, yet still causes this issue. I'm hoping this helps someone more knowledgeable realize which condition that might be.

title: kube_codegen.sh: validation-gen --readonly-pkg flags are passed as a single argument due to quoting
content:
What happened?

Encountered when working on https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137349. 

In kube_codegen.sh, the --readonly-pkg flags for validation-gen are wrapped in double quotes:

 

Double-quoting $() suppresses word splitting, so the entire output becomes a single argument to the generator.

pflag.StringSliceVar silently stores the whole string as one entry that never matches any package, so readonly protection is a no-op for out-of-tree projects using kube_codegen.sh.

title: histograms: Handle unknown schemas gracefully and more robust
content: Proposal

title: histograms: Handle unknown schemas gracefully and more robust
content:
With the upcoming custom buckets, we'll introduce the first schema that isn't a "standard" exponential schema. More will follow, because it is easy to add more custom schemas once the plumbing is in place. Once histogram with those new schemas are being produced, they will eventually slip into a program that isn't aware of them yet.

But even without custom schemas, histograms with unexpected schemas could be produced by accident (programming error, data corruption).

title: histograms: Handle unknown schemas gracefully and more robust
content:
We have some checks in place to not ingest histograms with unknown schemas, but there will be plenty of paths that aren't covered, known and unknown. (E.g. you run a newer Prometheus version with NHCB support. Then you have to downgrade, but the NHCB histograms are in storage.)

Fundamentally, the code should be able to handle an encounter with unknown schemas gracefully.

Example for the current (pre-NHCB) implementation of the method:

title: histograms: Handle unknown schemas gracefully and more robust
content:
Example for the current (pre-NHCB) implementation of the method:
- Negative schemas are simply assumed to continue as standard exponential schemas forever. So a NHCB histogram (schema -53) would silently result in extremely large bucket boundaries (in the best case as this is obviously wrong and easiest to spot).
- With a positive schema greater than 8, the program will panic because we hit the end of the precalculated slice.

title: histograms: Handle unknown schemas gracefully and more robust
content: We don't want the program to panic, but we also don't want to silently return bogus bucket boundaries. If we don't want to add error returns everywhere, maybe we could return as bucket boundaries for unknown schemas? This could be handled further up in the call stack as needed.

title: File descriptor leak on early return in writeMetaFile (tsdb/block.go)
content:
What did you do?

In tsdb/block.go (function writeMetaFile), a file descriptor is opened with:

 f, err := os.Create(tmp)

There is an early return path that exits without closing f:

 return 0, err

Other error paths in the same function correctly use:

 return 0, errors.Join(err, f.Close())

but this specific early-return path does not close the file.

If the error condition triggers, the file descriptor is leaked.

What did you expect to see?

title: File descriptor leak on early return in writeMetaFile (tsdb/block.go)
content:
What did you expect to see?

The file descriptor should be closed on all return paths.

Specifically, the early-return path should mirror the existing pattern used elsewhere in the function, e.g.:

 return 0, errors.Join(err, f.Close())

This ensures no file descriptor leak occurs on error.

What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

An early-return path exits without closing the file descriptor.

title: File descriptor leak on early return in writeMetaFile (tsdb/block.go)
content:
An early-return path exits without closing the file descriptor.

If os.Create(tmp) succeeds but a subsequent operation fails before the explicit close is reached, the function returns without calling f.Close().

This results in a file descriptor leak on that error path.

Darwin arm64

title: OTLP Write：Error `storage.ErrTooOldSample` is now generating HTTP error 500 instead of HTTP error 400， client will retry
content:
Proposal

Remote Write: Error is now generating HTTP error 400 instead of HTTP error 500。so the client can not retry。

but for otlp write，storage.ErrTooOldSample is now generating HTTP error 500。

code

for python otlp sdk，
` @staticmethod
 def _retryable(resp: requests.Response) -> bool:
 if resp.status_code == 408:
 return True
 if resp.statuscode >= 500 and resp.statuscode <= 599:
 return True
 return False`

when client push a too old sample， it will retry。

A malicious query string with only continuation bytes could crash the Prometheus server via the before the query was parsed or validated.

title: tsdb: fix memory leaks in buffer pools
content:
Summary
- Fix memory leaks in TSDB buffer pools by clearing reference fields before returning buffers to pools
- Affected pools:
 - : clear field
 - : clear pointer
 - : clear pointer
 - : clear and strings

Without these fixes, the pooled buffers retain references to data that should be garbage collected, causing memory to accumulate over time.

This established pattern, of clearing references before pool re-use, is already followed in , and . The former two were fixed in #13712.
```

title: [Prometheus Engine] Race condition / inconsistency causing broken native histograms
content:
What did you do?

We're using the Prometheus Engine as a library, in a slightly customized form (no impact regarding the bug), and noticed inconsistent behaviour / a race condition for metrics making use of the "native histogram" implementation.

title: [Prometheus Engine] Race condition / inconsistency causing broken native histograms
content:
We have our own implementation, that returns native histogram samples for certain queries. We use the to set fixed bucket values, where we provide an array that gets re-used. According to godoc, this should be allowed:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
(https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/blob/1a190d36933e49b95438657f7241d2a56ae79012/model/histogram/float_histogram.go#L59)

title: [Prometheus Engine] Race condition / inconsistency causing broken native histograms
content:
But we noticed in a different place, the slice actually gets modified: https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/blob/1a190d36933e49b95438657f7241d2a56ae79012/model/histogram/float_histogram.go#L122

Looking at the stack-trace from the race condition, it shows this method gets called with a previously cached instance, that was stored on a ring buffer:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: [Prometheus Engine] Race condition / inconsistency causing broken native histograms
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

In our case, because we're passing in a re-used slice, that original slice afterwards contains modified values.
But I've also seen the race condition get triggered for other metric requests, so I believe its a generic problem and at least doesn't align with the docs on .

What did you expect to see?

No modifications to the slice

What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

title: [Prometheus Engine] Race condition / inconsistency causing broken native histograms
content:
What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

See above. The slice gets internally modified.
Instead, I think always a new instance needs to be created, unless checking if values are equal would be 'cheaper'.
But at least different values should not overwrite one another.

Note; I'm happy to provide a fix as we have this resolved in our fork. Just not sure if always creating a new slice is fine, or if there are proposals to fix it in a different way.

title: `histogram_fraction` returns NaN for classic histograms when `lower` falls within the first bucket
content:
What did you do?

 returns when the argument falls within the first bucket of a classic histogram. For example, for a classic histogram with the following buckets, returns , instead of the expected . The same query on a native histogram with custom buckets (NHCB) returns the correct result.
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: `histogram_fraction` returns NaN for classic histograms when `lower` falls within the first bucket
content: , which calculates fractions for classic histograms, always assumes as the lower bound of the first bucket, then tries to interpolate the number of observations within that bucket. This leads to dividing infinity by infinity and thus yields . In contrast, , which calculates fractions for native histograms, assumes a lower bound of when the histogram has only positive buckets and produces the correct fraction.

title: `histogram_fraction` returns NaN for classic histograms when `lower` falls within the first bucket
content:
Similarly, for histogram with negative buckets where the first bucket’s lower bound is , returns when the argument falls within that first bucket. For example, for a classic histogram with the buckets below, returns instead of the expected .
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: `histogram_fraction` returns NaN for classic histograms when `lower` falls within the first bucket
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
 also returns in this case. However, I would expect the first bucket ( lower bound) to be treated similarly to the last bucket ( upper bound) and contribute no observations. For the last bucket ( upper bound), interpolation effectively yields 0 because the denominator is infinite while the numerator is finite:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
However, if the first bucket has a lower bound, interpolation becomes , which evaluates to , so interpolation should be skipped in this case.

title: `histogram_fraction` returns NaN for classic histograms when `lower` falls within the first bucket
content:
I've explored this issue and think I have a solution. If the maintainers agree this is indeed a bug, I'd be happy to open a PR.

What did you expect to see?

For histograms with only positive buckets, assume the first bucket's lower bound is . For histograms with negative buckets, no interpolation should be used for the first bucket ( lower bound).

What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

I saw instead.

title: promql(NHCB): histogram_stddev and histogram_stdvar should use arithmetic mean for NHCB
content:
Currently, and use the geometric mean of a bucket range as representative for the observations in that bucket. That makes sense for exponential buckets, but for NHCB, we should simply use the arithmetic mean (as we also use linear interpolation to estimate quantiles and fractions).

Example for the geometric mean in : https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/blob/b4d3c06acb805f9b9e786eb268b7cea84b34d1b0/promql/functions.go#L1371

This should be an easy change. Just do the arithmetic mean instead for NHCBs.

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content:
tl;dr: If we cut a new chunk because the schema or the zero threshold has changed in a "compatible" way, we will always set a counter reset header of "no counter reset" in the new chunk although we haven't really checked if there was a counter reset. Luckily, this isn't hurting us right now because we don't trust "no counter reset" in the counter reset header anyway (for other reasons).

More detailed explanation:

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content:
More detailed explanation:

First of all: What is a "compatible" change of the schema or the zero threshold? This happens when the instrumented target decides to get rid of some buckets by reducing the resolution and/or grow the zero bucket. The previous histograms (with the higher resolution and/or the smaller zero bucket) can be converted to the new schema and zero bucket width so the histograms are still compatible with each other for PromQL operations.

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content: The "gold standard" for counter reset detection is the FloatHistogram.DetectReset method. If it encounters a difference in schema and/or the zero threshold, it will find out if the change is compatible (see above) and will perform the conversion for the earlier histogram first, after which it will run the full reset detection (checking all bucket counts and the total count).

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content: During ingestion, though, we are mostly interested if we have to cut a new chunk at all. One of the reason is a counter reset, but any change in schema requires cutting a new chunk. So if there is a change in schema, the code quickly decides that a new chunk has to be cut, without further counter reset detection. This is fine, in principle, but the problem is that we set a "no counter reset" counter reset header of the new chunk in this case, which might or might not represent the truth

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content: . Instead, we either need to run a full counter reset detection or simply set a counter reset header of "unknown counter reset".

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content: Given that we currently do not trust a "no counter reset" header anyway (because the preceding chunk could get removed, which would invalidate the information anyway), this is not breaking anything right now, but we should still fix the behavior

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content: . I propose for now to just set "unknown counter reset" in this case because investing all the work in a full counter reset detection is in vain as long as we have no way of trusting a "no counter reset" header (which is something we might do in the future, see https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/15346 for ideas). Most likely, we should change the appendable method to return a rather than just a bool.

title: TSDB (histograms): Counter reset header might go undetected during ingest
content: @krajorama

title: [BUGFIX] PromQL: fix slice indexing bug in info function
content:
Which issue(s) does the PR fix:
Fixes https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/17134.
Slice indices were being used for matching between data and info series, but those indices are not stable - they may change from step to step in the presence of time series churn. Replaced indices with hashes for robust matching instead.
Also added more details to the error message to make diagnosing issues like this easier when using the function at scale.

title: Lots of "discovery manager scrape" errors message while reading local json files after 2.46.0 upgrade
content:
What did you do?

We have an external process that generate json target files regularly (every 10 min) from different sources. Those files are local to the Prometheus process and are overridden every 10 min.

This have been working flawlessly since a couple of year and I never saw anything complaining on the prometheus logs.

title: Lots of "discovery manager scrape" errors message while reading local json files after 2.46.0 upgrade
content:
After upgrading from to I started to see logs entry about And as we refresh the files very often (and we do have multiple files) it fill the log and make it harder to see


What did you expect to see?

Similar behavior of the file reading process

What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

Lots of log entry complaining about the file being used by another process

One of my concerns is that the is two possibilities:

title: Lots of "discovery manager scrape" errors message while reading local json files after 2.46.0 upgrade
content:
One of my concerns is that the is two possibilities:

- Prometheus have always had those “used by another process” errors but didn’t log them
- Something has changed that cause those new errors

The risk for us is to lose targets from time to time if Prometheus is not happy with the read process and fill the logs with useless messages (we do know those files are been rewritten regularly)

Missing a read one time should probably not be an error but a warning no?

title: Improve handling of staleness markers around hashmod sharding use cases
content: Proposal

title: Improve handling of staleness markers around hashmod sharding use cases
content:
Currently, scaling up or down a Prometheus or Prometheus Agent setup that makes use of Hashmod Sharding leads to staleness markers being inserted for series that get re-assigned to another shard (as the target disappears from its original owner's scrape pool).

This leads to gaps in graphs, even if the metric will re-appear in the next scrape interval.

title: Improve handling of staleness markers around hashmod sharding use cases
content:
It'd be nice if we could have some more fine-grained handling of staleness markers to avoid causing issues in environments who have some automated ways of dynamically scaling their Prometheus instances. (this is also relevant to scraping with Grafana Agent clustering).

I don't have a proposal ready here, so I wanted to start by asking: Has this come up in the past? Any ideas that we didn't have the time pursuing?

title: OTLP receiver: Generate `target_info` samples between the earliest and latest samples per resource
content: Modify the OTLP receiver to generate samples starting at the earliest and ending at the latest samples per resource, instead of only one for the latest timestamp. Between those samples, one sample is generated every lookback delta/2 timestamps. The idea behind generating samples spaced lookback delta/2 apart is to ensure that is found for every connected time series sample generated from the same request.

title: OTLP receiver: Generate `target_info` samples between the earliest and latest samples per resource
content:
The reason for adding a sample corresponding to the latest timestamp is that otherwise you may miss the latest when doing a join or function query, if you have to look back more than lookback delta/2 for the latest corresponding non-info sample.

The motivation behind this fix is that I realized when debugging strange PromQL function behaviour that the reason was that started one minute later than the time series it was being joined with (by ). See Grafana Mimir issue for reference.

title: OTLP receiver: Translation strategy `NoUTF8EscapingWithSuffixes` escapes some metric name characters when adding of metric suffixes is enabled
content:
What did you do?

The new OTLP receiver translation strategy is documented as "preserves all special characters like dots", but instead it does escape certain characters in metric names when adding of metric suffixes is enabled.

What did you expect to see?

I expect that the translation strategy corresponds to the name validation scheme, in allowing all valid UTF-8 characters, for both metric and label names.

What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

title: OTLP receiver: Translation strategy `NoUTF8EscapingWithSuffixes` escapes some metric name characters when adding of metric suffixes is enabled
content:
What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

Let's say you have an OTel gauge with the name "metric.+name+" and unit "°C". The translation logic, when adding of metric suffixes is enabled, will currently produce the Prometheus metric name "metric.name+°C", while the correct translation should instead be "metric.+name+_°C".

I've tested https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/pull/15440, but it only partially remedies the above case.

title: TSDB(histograms): possibility of counter reset overdetection
content: While discussing all the counter reset detection issues with overlapping chunks and out-of-order ingestion, I realized there is a rare edge case where a counter reset overdetection can happen even without any overlapping chunks or out-of-order ingestion. It just needs the later addition of an non-overlapping block to the TSDB:

title: TSDB(histograms): possibility of counter reset overdetection
content: The minimal case is a series ABCD where the counter reset happens in between B and C. However, only A and D are recorded first, with the counter reset detected between A and D. A and D happen to land in different blocks (not just different chunks). Later, B and C are backfilled and copied into the TSDB's data directory as a separate block, with a counter reset detected between B and C.

title: TSDB(histograms): possibility of counter reset overdetection
content: This results in the following: Each sample went into its own chunk (but of course, there might be real life cases with many samples in each of those chunks). The chunk containing C and D is each marked with a counter reset hint of (i.e. "for sure there was a counter reset happening with this sample"). If the series is queried later, both of those counter reset hints will be propagated, so PromQL sees a counter reset for both C and D, although it should only see it for C.

title: TSDB(histograms): possibility of counter reset overdetection
content: So far, we concluded that whenever any overlapping blocks or overlapping chunks get into the game, we need to return whenever we switch from "one stream of samples to another". In this case, however, we have no way of noticing that the block containing B and C was inserted later, because there is just no overlap in blocks or chunks.

title: TSDB(histograms): possibility of counter reset overdetection
content: I think the easiest way out is to make sure that the 1st chunk in a block always gets a counter reset hint of . I believe this has to be persisted in the chunk itself, not just set during querying, because blocks can get compacted, and then we will "forget" that a chunk was once the 1st in a block. (In the example above, the two old blocks and the newly inserted one might all got compacted into one block.)

title: Potential Race condition when compacting head and receiving samples at the same time
content:
We run cortex and we are seeing some blocks with samples out of order or duplicated samples. Looking into the issue seems that tsdb is accepting those samples due some race condition.

https://github.com/cortexproject/cortex/issues/4573 has a detailed explanation what we see on our side but basically seems we have a race condition when receiving samples for a series that is being GC'ed after head compaction.

Ex Timeline:
* SeriesA had samples from 22:00 to 23:50

title: Potential Race condition when compacting head and receiving samples at the same time
content:
Ex Timeline:
* SeriesA had samples from 22:00 to 23:50
* At 1:30 u compact a new block (with data up to 00:00)
* We receive a new sample (t1, v1) for SeriesA and the push go routineA is on this line
* At the same point the SeriesA is gced here and here
* routineA resume and (t1, v1) is pushed to the store (even though the series was gc'ed already)
* We receive another sample (t1, v2), we create the SeriesA in memory and allow this push. 

What did you do?

title: Potential Race condition when compacting head and receiving samples at the same time
content:
What did you do?

Run Cortex and send very sparse data with duplicated samples. It will eventually happen.

Repro case (Unit test): https://github.com/alanprot/prometheus/commit/e30d8710b1e7ad2fbc6b2d3e0d59f76a58ba6807

Maybe a potential fix could be something like: https://github.com/alanprot/prometheus/commit/fbc420637e52c83cd074bf5d77c4b643f681bf83

What did you expect to see?

Duplicates samples should not be ingested and shipped to the block.

title: Potential Race condition when compacting head and receiving samples at the same time
content:
Duplicates samples should not be ingested and shipped to the block.

What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

Duplicated samples are ingested.

Environment
Cortex but it seems that the problem can happen in prometheus as well.

title: otlp/translator: Use separate function for metric names with UTF8 characters
content:
After many discussions with the team, it seems that our previous approach of using the method to translate all kinds of translation strategies stretched its usefulness too much. 

This is an alternative for https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/pull/15440. Here, concerns are split into two separate functions. was renamed to and focuses only on building metric names that follow Prometheus naming conventions.

title: otlp/translator: Use separate function for metric names with UTF8 characters
content:
The new function is responsible for building metric names that keep characters as they come. The only thing it does is append prefixes and suffixes, if asked.

For the reviewers, I recommend reviewing commits individually :)

title: otlp/translator: Use separate function for metric names with UTF8 characters
content:
Fixes https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/15534
Fixes https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/issues/15362

title: OTLP receiver: Allow colons in non-standard units
content:
Modify the classical mode OTel metric name translation logic so colons are allowed in non-standard units. On inspecting the code, I noticed that a mistake had been made previously in not allowing this character (i.e. the label name character set was applied, not the metric name character set).

Corresponding tests are added, plus one for the case where the OTel "per" unit is completely invalid.

title: Relabeling default regex does not match newlines
content:
The default regex for relabeling does not support newlines .

We can consider this a bug and fix it in the next minor release, or fix this in prometheus 3.

title: Fix: OTLP translator now keeps multiple separators in metric names
content:
Fixes #15362 


To solve this, I've refactored the function that splits metric names into tokens and separators. Now, the separators are inferred from the position gaps between tokens.

~The fix looks easy, but I've hit a strange behavior with foreign characters. Foreign characters skip positions when looping over an array of runes (a string). As shown in this playground: https://go.dev/play/p/qYywzhbbXcf Notice how the positions of and do not follow the numeral order.~ Fixed in the second commit :)

title: Fix bug in `lbl!~".+"` postings for matchers shortcut
content: We were appending to the wrong slice, so instead of removing values, we were adding them.

title: rules.NewGroup: Fix when no logger is passed
content: Fixing a bug in , where it fails to use a by default. This causes segfaults in Grafana Mimir. Including a test for this case.

title: Opening TSDB ReadOnly can corrupt TSDB (non-sequential files)
content: When we Open TSDB in read only, programmatically, we should still take the lock. Not doing so often leads to "un-sequential" mmap files errors, if Prometheus is still running. I think that this behavior is not correct and that we should take the lock to prevent that kind of issues.

 and @codesome you both authored that PR

title: scrape: stop erroring on empty scrapes
content:
Fixes #15337 (along with PR #15352 for ).

Empty scrapes should not cause an error (from lack of ) or fail to append a stale marker.

Existing tests have been updated to stop defaulting to "text/plain" except in cases where no header is provided and the fallback protocol has to be specified explicitly. Lots of churn in the tests but the previous fallback setting was masking these problems. Now only the specific tests that don't supply a have fallback specified.)

New tests added to cover negative (non-blank scrape with no header) and positive cases (blank scrape with no ).

title: bugfix: Fix otlp translator switching colons to underscores in suffix adding mode
content:
Fixes #15199 

Colons are allowed in metric names even without UTF8 support. The usage of colons is a very common pattern for recording rules, as documented in our website and by PromLabs, and broadly used by popular mixins.

This bug only occurs in suffix adding mode, not otherwise. Note that this mode is hard coded in Prometheus itself, but can be disabled when using Prometheus as a library.

title: Remote_write unable to send due to `segments are not sequential` error
content: When I look at the wal directory I can see that I have in the wal directory. This means in the listSegments call it is skipped, meaning it reads 6 then 8 and then complains about this error. I then attempted to restart prometheus (thinking it would re-write the WAL or finish the repair) -- but unfortunately it now won't start -- with the same error ( ). So this means that now I can't do anything with prometheus until I manually clear the WAL file -- which seems not-so-good :)

title: Remote_write unable to send due to `segments are not sequential` error
content:
So with that in mind, it seems that we should probably add some option (env var, cli flag) to allow for partial WAL files -- otherwise if any file is corrupt the only way to get prometheus to start is to delete them all or manually rename all the files.

Environment

* Prometheus version: 2.12.0

title: Make rate possible non-counter annotation consistent
content:
Previously a PossibleNonCounterInfo annotation would be left in cases where a range-vector selects 1 float data point, even if no more points are selected in order to calculate a rate.

This change ensures an output float exists before emitting such an annotation.

This fixes an inconsistency where a series with mixed data (ie, a float and a native histogram) would emit an annotation without any points.

For example,

```
title: PromQL: Parentheses should work around any node type, in all contexts
content: EDIT: This should be a syntax error instead of being allowed.

title: PromQL: Parentheses should work around any node type, in all contexts
content:
ORIGINAL:

Parentheses should always just act like a no-op.

But they lead to query errors when used around some function parameters, like:

 rate((foo[5m]))

...or:

 label_join(foo, ("foo"), "bar")

This leads to errors like:

 Error executing query: unexpected error: interface conversion: promql.Expr is promql.ParenExpr, not promql.StringLiteral

title: PromQL: Parentheses should work around any node type, in all contexts
content: The reason is that some function implementations assume concrete implementation types, like here: https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/blob/64194f7d45cbb7fa2f41c8f219706308f65d511a/promql/functions.go#L66 and https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/blob/64194f7d45cbb7fa2f41c8f219706308f65d511a/promql/functions.go#L754-L755.

title: PromQL: Parentheses should work around any node type, in all contexts
content: Currently we don't have any ways of generating non-literal strings or matrices without matrix selectors, so I think we only need to ensure that parentheses are skipped around these child nodes before trying to use them.

title: Range selectors with an `@` modifier are wrongly scoped in range queries.
content:
What did you do?

While helping out with a discussion about shoehorning Prometheus into forecasting, I played with the modifier and .

The idea is the following: If I peg the range selector used as the 1st argument of to a certain time, it should be possible to use meaningfully in a range query, e.g.

 .

What did you expect to see?

Good question. There are different outcomes I would consider reasonable:

title: Range selectors with an `@` modifier are wrongly scoped in range queries.
content:
In any case, I expect to calculate the same slope no matter what the evaluation time is. (And if I run , I indeed see a constant value across the time range.)

What will it do with that slope? Since I used as the 2nd argument, it would be fair if it completely ignored the slope and just evaluated normally. (Graphing the range query would look the same as graphing directly.)

title: Range selectors with an `@` modifier are wrongly scoped in range queries.
content: Another option could be that it would take the end of the range selector and extrapolated the value there all the way to the evaluation time using the calculated slope. (Graphing the range query would yield a straight line with the slope as calculated with .)

title: Range selectors with an `@` modifier are wrongly scoped in range queries.
content:
A third option would be that it would somehow see the end of the range selector as the new "now", so it would simply take that value, without extrapolation as the 2nd argument is . (Graphing the range query would constantly show the value of at the end of the range selector.)


What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

I saw neither of the three options above, but instead something like this:

title: Range selectors with an `@` modifier are wrongly scoped in range queries.
content:
Weirdly, if I extend the range of the range query, the graph moves somewhere else:



And to add even more weirdness, when I run an instant query, a value is returned for "now", i.e. a time where the graph view showed no value.

title: Range selectors with an `@` modifier are wrongly scoped in range queries.
content: So for sure, something is going very wrong here because the range query should yield the same result as the instant query for the evaluation time of the instant query. But I also do not understand the result of the range query. The notion of "now" seems to be broken there.

title: Range selectors with an `@` modifier are wrongly scoped in range queries.
content:
First, we need to find out what semantics we desire in the first place (see expectations above – I currently believe the second one is the most useful). Then we have to ensure that the implementation follows that expectation.
```

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
Is this the right place to submit this?

- [x] This is not a security vulnerability or a crashing bug
- [x] This is not a question about how to use Istio

Bug Description

Background: I'm working of designing changes to the metric reporting in ambient to account for E/W gateways used in multi-network deployment. You can find the design document (maybe, WIP) here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1gyDsViXTHlozTbHFuggNmYFrL6P3om4ZvTMdy_-Ul74/edit?usp=sharing.

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
One of the decisions that we made and agreed on is that E/W gateway will be transparent for both L4 and L7 metrics. Currently it's not exactly the case, that's why I'm filing this bug.

For example, in waypoint, when we select an E/W gateway endpoint, we use the E/W gateway IP to lookup peer metadata and it so happens that we actually have a WDS entry for E/W gateway IP and will use it to report peer metadata.

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content: This metadata will be incomplete (because E/W gateway WDS entry is not based on an actual workload, but instead is based on a gateway resource which hides some details), but the issue is that we report it all. Given that we made a decision to make E/W gateway transparent to L4 and L7 metrics, xDS-based metadata discovery mechanism should treat E/W gateway IP as unknown.

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
A similar problem will occur when waypoints receive a request from the E/W gateway (though in this case we will have an actual workload). In this case they should not report in the metric labels E/W gateway metadata, but instead the metadata of the workload behind it.

Regardless of how we want to close the metrics reporting gap in ambient multi-network deployments, given the decision, the current behavior is not correct and should be fixed.

Version

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
Version

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Additional Information

We discussed a bit how we can address this issue and basically there are few parts:

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
1. When waypoints selects an E/W gateway endpoint
 * for this case, in pilot we can add a piece of endpoint metadata for E/W gateway endpoints that will indicate to the xDS-based peer metadata discovery filter to ignore this endpoint and report it as if it didn't find metadata at all

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
* Other options we considered: add an explicit note to WDS to indicate that it's an E/W gateway entry and use that in peer metadata discovery; derive that endpoint is an E/W gateway by comparing it's network to local network and use that
2. When waypoint receives an incoming connection from an E/W gateway

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
2. When waypoint receives an incoming connection from an E/W gateway
 * for this case, the suggestion is that client that creates the HBONE connection and knows whether the connection goes to the E/W gateway or not, can put a header into HBONE CONNECT request to indicate that this request went to an E/W gateway - waypoint can extract that from the inner-HBONE and act accordingly

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
* ztunnels currently detect that they received a request from an E/W gateway by looking at the authority in the request, it seems like header provided by the client is a bit more explicit way to communicate the same, but it provides an alternative to be considered
3. ztunnels, I think, report E/W gateway as a destination in L4 metrics - if indeed the case, that should be fixed as well.

title: xDS-based metadata discovery reports incorrect labels for multi-network deployments
content:
When ztunnels recieve a connection from E/W gateway, as I mentioned above, they can actually tell that it's a connection from the E/W gateway, but I'm not sure what metric labels we report in this case (I think we report source as unknown for everything or almost everything), so it seems like we are more or less good in this case, though still worth checking.

Related issue: #58476

+

is to query "Version status of all Envoys connected to this Pilot instance".

title: istioctl fancy icons may not render on all terminals
content:
fix or revert #50744 so that reported issues don't make it into 1.23

proposed fix would be to revert back to the original success/fail characters and decorate the end of each line with a fancy trailing emoji so that if the character isn't present in a user's font they are still able to tell what happened. This seems more reliable than making some sort of attempt to determine what font is being used and then attempting to figure out if that font contains all the characters we're going to use.

title: [BUG] CLI compose up and down hangs with ttyWriter
content:
I have attached the full SIGQUIT trace showing the deadlock.

My AI friend suggests a lock inversion between two goroutines:
1. The Main Thread is blocked in ( ) trying to close the UI.
2. The UI Thread is blocked in ( ) waiting to acquire the Mutex to redraw the terminal.
This collision happens most frequently when the UI is bombarded with state changes (e.g., many replicas finishing healthchecks simultaneously) combined with terminal redraw events ( ).

title: [BUG] Unhelpful warning "No services to build" with `docker compose up`
content:
Description

Commit c89b8a2 added a warning log "No services to build" when is run without any services to build.

This also affects even if no directive exists within . Running without the image present in the platform cache (and , which is default if unset), results in a pull from the directive (if possible) and starts the container(s).

Subsequent runs of with a cached image result in the unhelpful (albeit accurate) warning message "WARN[0000] No services to build".

title: [BUG] Unhelpful warning "No services to build" with `docker compose up`
content:
I imagine this behaviour is due to compose running as a fallback to , even if the image is cached and not specified. 

Running results in no warning as expected, but I would expect this to be default behaviour if is not specified and no directive exists.

Obviously, pre 5.0 behaviour had no such warning (even if the internal calls to were identical).

Steps To Reproduce

docker-compose.yml:
~~~yaml
services:
 app:
 image: hello-world:latest
~~~

title: [BUG] Unhelpful warning "No services to build" with `docker compose up`
content:
1. (assuming the image is not cached)
~~~bash
root@host /srv/myapp # docker compose up
[+] up 0/2
[+] up 4/4ello-world:latest [⠀] Pulling 2.0s

Attaching to app-1
app-1 |
app-1 | Hello from Docker!
app-1 | This message shows that your installation appears to be working correctly.
...
~~~

title: [BUG] Image mount holds on to stale image even once image is removed
content:
Description

Mounting an image which has been built in the same stack does not work as expected: rebuilds of the image do not cause containers which mount it to be recreated.

Since https://github.com/docker/compose/pull/12725 and https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/48798 it's possible to mount files from images. Docker warns it's still an experimental feature and there are open issues (e.g. https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/50999). So some problems are to be expected.

For some reason container b still mounts the old image, which shouldn't exist any more: I explicitly pruned builder and images and the images list only outputs bash and the new a image.

title: [BUG] No referrers-API backwards compatibility on "publish --app"
content:
Description

According the OCI Distribution spec v1.1 "Backwards compatibiltiy" section:

Client implementations MUST support registries that implement partial or older versions of the OCI Distribution Spec. This section describes client fallback procedures that MUST be implemented when a new/optional API is not available from a registry.

title: [BUG] No referrers-API backwards compatibility on "publish --app"
content:
Neither GitHub nor GitLab support the referrers API. So the current implementation of the feature is useless with these registries, because there is no way to recover the digest of the "referrer" (except analyzing verbose publish).

The specification describes how to detect lack of support for the referrers API.

And how to implement backwards compatibility over the so-called "Referrers tag schema".

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content:
Description

Docker at least since version 28.2.2, probably much longer seems to perform a weird routing table check before creating its own bridging network under linux. If the system has a routing table entry without a nexthop via address, docker seems to consider the entire ip prefix as unusable and will not start if all address-pools defined are somewhere within that prefix. It will print

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content: If the very same routing entry however exists with a nexthop ip address, docker starts and works normally and as expected. The issue here is that different VPN solutions create different routing entries depending on how they work internally. Our company is in the process of switching from one VPN solution to another and as is normal, you push ip routes to the vpn clients to "pull" the traffic to the company over the vpn

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content: . Now we have users where suddenly docker stops to work since we push a route to the private address space used in the company where a subnet is also configured as an address-pool withing docker's daemon.json.

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content: The issue is that docker apparently uses the linux routing table to determine whether adress-pools ip ranges are eligible but that information can never be gotten by reading the routing table. The routing table will always have "overlapping" routes simply because already the default route is shorthand for 0.0.0.0/0 which denotes the entire possible ipv4 address space, yet that does not declare all ip adresses as "used"

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content: . There is the concept of most specific route wins which is what routing actually is and the table is used for.

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content:
The bug is that docker seems to misuse the routing table information for some determination for which it cannot give the correct answer.

This bug causes issues on all users with VPNs where pushed routing information is preventing the use of address-pools and hence the entire bridge-network mechanism.

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content:
Note: some VPNs push a 0.0.0.0/1 and 128.0.0.0/1 route to signal to send all traffic over the vpn but not change the local user's default route (0.0.0.0) as these two entries are more specific and thus override it. In this case, there is no eligible address pool possible in docker, as due to the check and bug docker considers all ip address ranges in use.

A simplified variant in how to reproduce is added below.

Steps To Reproduce

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
[BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content:
`
sudo ip route add 10.0.0.0/8 dev eth0
`
4. restart docker. Docker daemon fails with "failed to start daemon: Error initializing network controller: error creating default "bridge" network: all predefined address pools have been fully subnetted"
5. remove the route again:
 
6. readd route with a via nexthop target:
`
sudo ip route add 10.0.0.0/8 via dev eth0
`
7. restart docker. Docker now works without complaining.

title: [BUG] docker misuses linux routing table to decide address-pool eligibility
content:

Docker Environment

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Anything else?

Fixes people's issues like in https://serverfault.com/questions/1187249/docker-on-a-vpn-all-predefined-address-pools-have-been-fully-subnetted

title: [BUG] Temporary file leak and race condition in "bridge convert"
content:
Description

The convert function in the bridge package creates a temporary compose.yaml file to be used by transformation containers but fails to remove it after execution. Furthermore, it uses a fixed filename in the system's temporary directory, leading to potential race conditions and execution failures on certain platforms (like macOS).

Steps To Reproduce

1. Run docker compose bridge convert --output ./out-test on any Compose project.

title: [BUG] Long notation of `volumes` `bind` mount doesn't apply selinux labels
content:
VOLUME: Can be either a host path on the platform hosting containers (bind mount) or a volume name.

-> When using an absolute or relative path like it's also a mount

2) See this documentation:

title: [BUG] `.env` evaluated during include of a base compose file when using `--env-file` with different file
content:
Description

I have a compose setup where I have a base compose file which is included by two other independent compose files, extending in different ways a service defined on the base compose file.

title: [BUG] `.env` evaluated during include of a base compose file when using `--env-file` with different file
content: The service on the base compose file requires some environment variables (via required interpolation) and each of the other two compose files are meant to be used with a separate env file, both defining the variables required by the base compose service while also including some variables specific to those compose files (also with required interpolation).

title: [BUG] `.env` evaluated during include of a base compose file when using `--env-file` with different file
content:
The problem is that if a file exists then compose evaluates it and fails if one of the required interpolations fails, even though we are launching compose with another env file through .

A workaround for this issue is either:

* Set the required variables in in all other env files
* Remove any required variable interpolation from the file
* Stop using by renaming it to something else

title: [BUG] container_name repeatedly prefixes with IDs on `failed` reconciliation

title: Support env-var interpolation for boolean-typed fields (e.g., depends_on.*.required) or provide a cast syntax
content:
Description

Today, Docker Compose variable interpolation substitutes strings before schema validation. When a field is typed as boolean (like services. .depends_on. .required), using ${VAR} causes a validation error because Compose sees a string, not a boolean:

 

This prevents toggling optional dependencies via env vars—an otherwise common and very useful pattern.

Why this matters

title: Support env-var interpolation for boolean-typed fields (e.g., depends_on.*.required) or provide a cast syntax
content:
Why this matters

depends_on.*.required is a boolean with default true (introduced around v2.20.x). It’s perfect for making a dependency optional when a service is gated by a flag or profile. But you can’t drive it from an env var today without preprocessing. 
Docker Documentation

Compose officially supports env-var interpolation from .env, shell, and --env-file, but the current behavior doesn’t allow those values to be typed when the schema expects booleans. 
Docker Documentation
+1

title: Support env-var interpolation for boolean-typed fields (e.g., depends_on.*.required) or provide a cast syntax
content:
Profiles are great (and we use them), but there are many real cases where you want a single compose file and a simple ENABLE_X=true/false switch, especially for CI jobs and local toggles. 
Docker Documentation

Minimal Reproducer

.env
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

compose.yaml
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Run
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Actual
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Expected
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
Proposal(s)

Any of the following would solve it without breaking existing configs:

title: Support env-var interpolation for boolean-typed fields (e.g., depends_on.*.required) or provide a cast syntax
content:
Any of the following would solve it without breaking existing configs:

Boolean coercion for typed fields
After interpolation, if a field is schema-typed as boolean, treat "true"/"false" (case-insensitive) as booleans; otherwise error.

Opt-in cast syntax
Add a cast operator for interpolation, e.g. ${ENABLEGRAFANA?bool} (or ${{ bool(ENABLEGRAFANA) }}), producing a true boolean, not a string.

title: Support env-var interpolation for boolean-typed fields (e.g., depends_on.*.required) or provide a cast syntax
content:
Honor YAML tag casts post-interpolation
Allow required: !!bool ${ENABLE_GRAFANA:-false} to be parsed as boolean. Currently this still errors because validation happens before/without a cast being applied.

Backward compatibility

Coerce only when the schema type is boolean; do not coerce in environment: (those are strings). This avoids historic yes/no pitfalls and keeps behavior predictable. Docs can note accepted values are exactly true|false. 
Docker Documentation

Environment

title: Support env-var interpolation for boolean-typed fields (e.g., depends_on.*.required) or provide a cast syntax
content:
Environment

Docker Compose: v2.34.0 (repro also reported by others since v2.20.x)

OS: Linux (x86_64)

Compose file uses the current Compose Specification. depends_on.required boolean is documented in service reference. 
Docker Documentation

Related docs

Interpolation: how env vars are injected into Compose files. 
Docker Documentation

Profiles: recommended for optional services (workaround, not a direct fix). 
Docker Documentation

title: Support env-var interpolation for boolean-typed fields (e.g., depends_on.*.required) or provide a cast syntax
content:
Service reference (notes required default/behavior). 
Docker Documentation

Impact

This unlocks simple, single-file toggles such as:

Optional observability stack (ENABLEGRAFANA, ENABLEPROMTAIL)

Optional seed/init jobs (REQUIRE_BOOTSTRAP)

Feature-gated infra bits in CI (USEMINIO, USELOCAL_REGISTRY)

Thanks for considering! Happy to test a PR or nightly build.

content: Description

title: [BUG] Internal DNS server randomly not working since v2.24.6
content:
Hello,
with Docker v25.0.3 and Docker Compose v2.24.6 (Debian Bookworm, up2date), i am experiencing random issues with the internal DNS server of docker.

The problem shows up, as the applications in the containers try to resolve the DNS name of another container - they are trying to send a DNS request to 127.0.0.11:53 and are getting a connection refused. The behavior is absolutely random, so i am not able to provide a way to reproduce it.

title: [BUG] Internal DNS server randomly not working since v2.24.6
content:
The behavior showed up on 5 servers - so basically on all servers in my setup, that got the update to Docker Compose v2.24.6.

With Docker Compose v2.24.5, the behavior doesnt show up on all of the mentioned systems. Downgrade + down and up on all docker compose stacks helped for me.

All services should build and start normally.

The command stops during the build stage with failed to execute bake: read |0: file already closed. No containers are started.

title: [BUG] The PWD environment variable will always be overwritten
content:
Description

With https://github.com/docker/compose/commit/f38f3f754ce32d6a640c39dcd41af126392986cd the environment variable will always be overwritten with the parent working directory using the Go function https://pkg.go.dev/os#Getwd.

This might be OK in 99% of all use cases, but this change breaks a special use case:

title: [BUG] The PWD environment variable will always be overwritten
content: I need to set the variable differently to match another directory structure within a VM that actually runs the Docker daemon. e.g. a Windows host system and a Debian guest system. The CLI tools with the plugin are installed on the Windows host and access the Docker daemon on the Debian guest. Directories are synced from the Windows host to the Debian host and therefore have another parent working directory in the context of the VM

title: [BUG] The PWD environment variable will always be overwritten
content:
Suggestion: Please check if a environment variable is present and only if it is not set it to the result of . That is a defensive programming technique and will satisfy both use cases.

This defect has been introduced with https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/tag/v2.35.0 with v2.34.0 it is still possible to set a specific variable.

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
Description

I'm running a deployment where the primary container is being created using docker compose up -d. The main container of the deployment is a service called . I'm spinning up temporary containers on a per-need basis using e.g .

When a temporary container has been created using and is still running, will enter the container instead of the container.

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
I have found various issues and PRs referencing this issue, and I've been able to narrow it down to being most likely connected with the way Docker Compose assigns the label and the label across various versions.

Related issues / PRs and history of changes:

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
* In 2.15.1, run containers are being incorrectly selected by exec (#10359)
* In v2.27.1, run containers are being incorrectly selected by exec (#11992)
* Starting with 2.29.1, run containers are supposed to be sorted at the end of the selection list so that they have lower priority than the app container (#11995). However, this sorting logic relies on the run container number being set.

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
* This seems to have stemmed from a decision to maintain the incorrect behavior in case users started relying on it - while still giving a higher priority to the managed service container(s) if they are running
	* I unfortunately haven't been able to test that the bug is NOT present when

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
* In v2.29.7, a user reports that run containers are being incorrectly selected by the option, which leads to realizing that run containers should probably not have a container number label to begin with (#12219).
* Starting in 2.30.0, run containers no longer have a container number (#12224)

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content: * this might be breaking the sorting logic introduced in #12219: in my understanding, if the container number is not set, the run container will be sorted before the app container (assuming will return 0 - and possibly an error which is being ignored in the sorting logic from #12219 - but I'm not familiar enough with Go to be 100% sure of this hypothesis).

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
End result: the regression is present again, and run containers are being selected by .

Steps To Reproduce

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

In another terminal window:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

 the above command enters the container.
 the above command enters the one-off "run" container.

Compose Version

Tried across three different Docker Compose versions:

Docker Compose version v2.18.1

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
Docker Compose version v2.18.1

* app container has a container number (set to 1)
* run container has a container number (set to 1)
* docker compose exec app bash enters the run container

Docker Compose version v2.21.0-desktop.1

* app container has a container number (set to 1)
* run container has a container number (set to 1)
* docker compose exec app bash enters the run container

Docker Compose version v2.39.1

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
Docker Compose version v2.39.1

* app container has a container number (set to 1)
 run container does not* have a container number
* docker compose exec app bash enters the run container

Potential fix proposals:

(Trying to help with brainstorming a possible fix now that I'm this deep into the investigation 😂)

1. Update sorting logic?

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
1. Update sorting logic?

Update the sorting logic from #12219 to always sort one-off containers which don't have a label after non-one-off containers which have a container number label?

The final sorting order used by would then be:
* managed containers sorted by container number (if any are present)
* one-off containers sorted in an undetermined order (if any are present)

2. Bring back container numbers on one-off containers and fix option?

title: [BUG] `docker compose exec` selects a one-off container instead of the service container
content:
2. Bring back container numbers on one-off containers and fix option?

This would mean essentially reverting #12224. This would enable the sorting logic to correctly consider the one-off container with as having the same number as the first service container, BUT in this case, the selection logic would need to be fixed in order to skip one-off containers.

3. Something else?

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content:
Description

When building an image via compose on its own, a fully cached build takes about one second, as expected. When built in the context of a test automation, the same fully cached build was consistently taking about thirty seconds (all expected output was displayed within a second, but just 'hung' after that). I finally determined the difference was an environment variable file.

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

[CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

I would not expect an environment file to influence the build when it's not used/applicable to the build.

Steps To Reproduce

Compose file ( ):

Environment file ( ):
