content:
JSON format makes it easier to see where the delay is; the standalone build (no environment file) takes about one second:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Specifying an environment file containing an OTEL-related value (intended for containers) takes ten seconds (.3 seconds to actually build, the remainder spent being 'stuck'):
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content: In my summary I said I was witnessing delays around thirty seconds; I suspect was largely responsible, but the lag was not consistent (I'm sure related to whether that endpoint is active). But the one I used above was very consistent, no doubt due to a ten second timeout somewhere.

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content:
Also, this used a non-standard file to make it easier to demonstrate (and also reflects my actual scenario), but the behavior is the same whether it's the default or custom (like my example) or if it's specified via or .

Compose Version

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Docker Environment

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Anything else?

This has similarities to #12998 (but may be just that... similar).

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content:
This has similarities to #12998 (but may be just that... similar).

This is specific to bake (i.e., this does not happen with ), but I don't think it is bake's fault. I'm pretty sure I know where the problem is so I didn't try older versions, but I did try with main with the same results.

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content:
It looks like anything passed via an environment file is added to the environment of compose itself
https://github.com/docker/compose/blob/f266715dd0310f1020b195443bf6cafe1922f313/cmd/compose/compose.go#L687-L693

and though some additions and removals are done, it's more-or-less kept intact
https://github.com/docker/compose/blob/f266715dd0310f1020b195443bf6cafe1922f313/pkg/compose/shellout.go#L33-L59

before passing it to the buildx plugin/process.

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content:
before passing it to the buildx plugin/process.

To complicate things, that near-verbatim passing of environment file to buildx (in #11974) was intentional:
also restore support for configuring buildx using env var set in .env

which I assume means the overriding of bake variables, not configuring buildx behavior itself.

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content: This seems more like an oversight or unintended consequence rather than a true bug. But it goes against expectations... I wouldn't expect an environment variable intended to control how my OTEL container is used to be (indirectly) used to build the images itself.

title: [BUG] Delays in build time when environment files have OTEL-related values and built with bake
content: Note: if the environment itself had those OTEL values, I would expect (or at least not be surprised) if they trickled down to buildx. But they are not in my environment, but in a file that the docs say are used only to replace values in a compose file or other environment files.

title: [BUG] docker compose images shows incorrect CREATED date ("292 years ago") because LastTagTime is unset
content:
Description

When running , the column displays absurd values such as “292 years ago” instead of the actual image build time.

This happens because Compose is using the field from image metadata, which defaults to if the image was pulled or built without a local tag event. The underlying image config has a valid field, but this are ignored.

Expected behaviour:

docker compose images should display the actual build/creation time of the image, based on the image’s Created field

Actual behaviour:

title: [BUG] docker compose images shows incorrect CREATED date ("292 years ago") because LastTagTime is unset
content:
Actual behaviour:

docker compose images shows 292 years ago because it renders Metadata.LastTagTime, which defaults to 0001-01-01T00:00:00Z unless explicitly set.

Cause

In cmd/compose/images.go, compose sets the Created column to i.LastTagTime:

imageList = append(imageList, img{
 ContainerName: ctr,
 ID: i.ID,
 Repository: i.Repository,
 Tag: i.Tag,
 Platform: platforms.Format(i.Platform),
 Size: i.Size,
 LastTagTime: i.LastTagTime,
})

This was introduced in commit ea1c26d.

title: [BUG] docker compose images shows incorrect CREATED date ("292 years ago") because LastTagTime is unset

This was introduced in commit ea1c26d.

title: [BUG] docker compose ignores update_config
content:
Description

Docker Compose ignores in and restarts container instead

Description
When a container fails during an update, Docker Compose ignores the setting in and continuously restarts the failing container instead of performing a rollback to the previous version.

 
According to the Compose Deploy Specification, when is set, Docker Compose should roll back to the previous container version if the update fails.

title: [BUG] Docker Compose build.args are ignored, but default ARG in Dockerfile works
content:
Description

Current Behavior
When running docker compose build, any build.args defined in the docker-compose.yml file are completely ignored during the build process.

title: [BUG] Docker Compose build.args are ignored, but default ARG in Dockerfile works
content: The build process behaves as if the variables are empty strings. This causes the FROM instruction in the Dockerfile, which depends on these args, to use an incorrect image tag (e.g., it falls back to :latest instead of using the specified version). This behavior occurs even when the argument values are hardcoded as strings in the docker-compose.yml file.

title: [BUG] Docker Compose build.args are ignored, but default ARG in Dockerfile works
content:
Expected Behavior
The build process should receive the variables (OLSVERSION and LSPHPVERSION) passed from the args section of the docker-compose.yml file. It should then use these values to correctly construct the base image tag in the FROM instruction (e.g., litespeedtech/openlitespeed:1.8.1-lsphp74).

Steps To Reproduce

1. Create the following files:

.env

title: [BUG] Docker Compose build.args are ignored, but default ARG in Dockerfile works
content:
- Running docker compose config correctly shows the interpolated values from the .env file, proving that Compose is reading the configuration as expected.
- Building the image directly with docker build --build-arg OLS_VERSION=1.8.1 ... works perfectly. This strongly suggests the issue lies within Docker Compose's mechanism for passing arguments to the Docker daemon.

title: [BUG] Docker Compose build.args are ignored, but default ARG in Dockerfile works
content: - The only functional workaround is to set default values directly in the Dockerfile (ARG OLS_VERSION=1.8.1), which defeats the purpose of using build arguments for dynamic configuration.

content:
Description

Docker writes this rule into the firewall table.

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
This has the effect of rewriting the source IPv6 address of all outbound packets, whatever service generates them.

Docker should not be masquerading packets that have nothing to do with it.

I have a server with several IPv6 addresses, including and . is used for most things, but my mail server uses . SPF and other email checks are based on mail using this address.

title: [BUG] Over-broad ip6tables masquerade rule
content:
The above rule, added by Docker, masquerades all IPv6 packets to have a source address of . That makes the email I send undeliverable.

This rule should not be added by Docker.

Steps To Reproduce

1. Have a machine with multiple IPv6 addresses, have different services bound to different Ipv6 addresses.
2. Install Docker.
3. Watch as packets from other services are rewritten.

title: [BUG] Docker Compose --wait not always honoring healthy healthcheck for container that crashed, then became healthy

Description

It seems like occasionally when a container is unhealthy on start and restarts and becomes healthy, the docker compose up -d --wait will fail with an unhealthy error message. This happens when docker compose up -d --wait is run in parallel, and with the policy restart: unless-stopped. Note that this occasionally happens, not all the time.

title: Docker compose up produces unexpected events
content:
Description of the issue

I had some problems with traefik and I noticed that the events that are sent when a container is started or created are not sent correctly. 

In my project I have 11 services. I start all the databases, keycloak and traefik (reverse-proxy) first. Then I start the rest of the services.

Although 3 of them are not detected in traefik, even though all configuration is correct.

title: Docker compose up produces unexpected events
content:
I had a look in the docker events command and recorded those events that happened when the docker-compose project started. 

Looking through the logs, I can see that several services are not logged being created (they didn't exist before), they are only logged starting. Another services is not even logged at all, but it is created and started, just not detected by traefik, because it listens to those events.

title: [BUG] Building with Bake fails if using context with TLS endpoint
content:
Description

I'm using Docker context with TLS endpoint.
When building with Compose (Bake enabled), I get this error:
 

I would put my 2 cents on PR #12961's commit:
It explicitly sets the environment variable from the CLI, always pointing to the context's endpoint.
 has a higher precedence than (which is also copied from the CLI).
Without proper TLS configuration (missing and ), Bake will try to connect to the endpoint without TLS, hence the error.

Steps To Reproduce

title: [BUG] Building with Bake fails if using context with TLS endpoint

title: Login Authentication Failed WebUI (Page Reload)
content:
Describe the bug
When you freshly created a new dedicated vault, the login in the webui is broken under circumstances.

title: Login Authentication Failed WebUI (Page Reload)
content:
To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a new dedicated vault
2. Login with the default credentials (admin token) and create a new "userpass" authentication along with the proper credentials
3. Now logout with your admin and login with the new user created, everything is fine (so I've just tested the admin token login and the login with the new user, both are working as intended)

title: Login Authentication Failed WebUI (Page Reload)
content: 4. If you again logout now and try to sign in again (regardless wheter it's the root admin or a new user created you are trying to login again with), your browser page window document reload mechanic messes up the login.

title: Login Authentication Failed WebUI (Page Reload)
content:
Why?

If you switch from the login method "Userpass" to "Token", the username is getting automatically pasted into the namespace field after the page reload has happened.

As soon as you edit the namespace to fix that, the page reloads another time and defaults to the last login method used (e.g. "Userpass") making it a death loop and unavailable to login again with another auth method (e.g. admin token).

title: Login Authentication Failed WebUI (Page Reload)
content:
Expected behavior
Either no page reload upon switching auth methods or editing the namespace or at least no automatically copying and pasting of credentials into the namespace field.

Why?

As soon as the system reloads upon a namespace change for example, the login is messed up due to error messages reading 
"Error Authentication failed: no handler for route"

This is a design flaw resulting in a logic inability to use your product.

title: Login Authentication Failed WebUI (Page Reload)
content:
This is a design flaw resulting in a logic inability to use your product.

Environment:
* Vault Server Version (retrieve with ): latest
* Vault CLI Version (retrieve with ): latest
* Server Operating System/Architecture: your web interface

Vault server configuration file(s):

Why would you require this if it is reproducable for everyone at every time

Additional context

Been waiting for your fix.

Workaround meanwhile:
- Delete Cookies & Cache
- Use incognito browser window

title: Vault UI shows "Renew token" button and warning even when renew-self is denied
content:
Describe the bug
Vault UI shows "Renew token" button and warning even when renew-self is denied

title: Vault UI shows "Renew token" button and warning even when renew-self is denied
content:
To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. In Vault, create OIDC role with a fixed token lifetime, with no pissibility of renewal:
vault write auth/oidc/role/default \
 allowedredirecturis="https://vault.example.com/ui/vault/auth/oidc/callback" \
 user_claim="sub" \
 groups_claim="groups" \
 policies="default" \
 token_ttl="3h" \
 tokenmaxttl="3h" \
 tokenexplicitmax_ttl="3h" \
 token_period="0s"

title: Vault UI shows "Renew token" button and warning even when renew-self is denied
content:
2. In the policy (default or another one that the user has), deny "renew-self":
path "auth/token/renew-self" {
 capabilities = ["deny"]
}

3. Authorize via OIDC in Vault UI

4. Leave the UI page open at least ~3min

5. After some time, a warning appears:"Session will expireWe've stopped auto-renewing your token due to inactivity. It will expire in about 3 hours on XXXX, XX:XX:XX PM GMTXX."

title: Vault UI shows "Renew token" button and warning even when renew-self is denied
content:
Expected Behavior
If "auth/token/renew-self" is deny, the Vault UI:
should not show the Renew token button, or
should show it in a disabled state, or

should perform an authorization check before showing the button.

If the token is not renewable (has no token_period or is denied renew by policy), the UI should respect this and not offer renew, so as not to confuse the user.

Environment:
* Vault Server Version (retrieve with ): v1.15.2
* Vault CLI Version (retrieve with ): v1.5.3

title: Sudden error when trying to authenticate in Vault UI via OIDC provider
content:
Hi,

I’m suddenly having a OIDC login issue in the UI after weeks of normal operation with Vault 1.12.2. 

I click on “Sign in with OIDC provider”, and almost immediately the main window shows “Error: The provider window was closed before authentication was complete. Please click Sign In to try again.”.

title: Sudden error when trying to authenticate in Vault UI via OIDC provider
content:
Then even though authentication to the OIDC identity provider (within the pop-up window) is successful, the pop-up shows message "Error: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'postMessage')", and Vault does not follow-through with the flow.

BUT, the OIDC workflow when initiated via the CLI works just fine. I get the OIDC provider URL, load it in the browser, authenticate, get the local URL, call the URL locally with curl, get the token and login with the token in the UI just fine.

title: Sudden error when trying to authenticate in Vault UI via OIDC provider
content:
The problem surfaced suddenly, there was no changes to configuration or node restarts. The only thing I tried was to demote the active to elect another, but that didn't make any difference (unsurprisingly).

All browsers fail, tested Brave, Chrome, Firefox, Safari, using “incognito” windows, so it’s not browser specific, extension related or pop-up blocker related. Issue affects other people as well, so not specific to my computer.

title: Sudden error when trying to authenticate in Vault UI via OIDC provider
content:
Can’t see anything interesting in the logs, and I run with DEBUG level.

Seems like a bug, because I've had this configuration running for more than a year, over multiple versions of Vault, including latest. Any suggestion on things to check?

title: ACME new-nonce endpoint: Replay-Nonce header set in handler but missing from HTTP response
content:
Describe the bug

The ACME endpoint (GET/HEAD) does not return the header to the client, even though the PKI handler sets it in the logical response. As a result, RFC 8555–compliant ACME clients that obtain the initial nonce via HEAD/GET to the newNonce URL (e.g. acme.sh) fail with "Could not get nonce."

To Reproduce

1. Configure Vault PKI with ACME enabled and a role (e.g. ) with EAB.
2. From any host that can reach Vault (including from inside the Vault pod), request the new-nonce URL:

title: ACME new-nonce endpoint: Replay-Nonce header set in handler but missing from HTTP response
content:
GET:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
 or from inside the pod:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

 HEAD: same URL with (curl) or wget's .

3. Observe the response headers.

Expected behavior (RFC 8555 §7.2)

- For HEAD: response MUST include a header with a fresh nonce (status 200 or 204).
- For GET: response MUST include and status 204 (No Content).

Actual behavior

- Response is (or for HEAD) with headers such as:
 - 
 - 
 - 
- No (or ) header is present.

title: ACME new-nonce endpoint: Replay-Nonce header set in handler but missing from HTTP response
content:
This occurs when calling Vault directly (e.g. from the pod), so it is not caused by a reverse proxy.

Handler vs HTTP layer

In , correctly sets the header:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

So the bug appears to be in the API/HTTP layer: headers from the logical response are not being written to the HTTP response for this (and possibly other ACME) path(s).

Impact

title: ACME new-nonce endpoint: Replay-Nonce header set in handler but missing from HTTP response
content:
Impact

- Clients that rely on GET/HEAD to new-nonce to obtain the first nonce (e.g. acme.sh, used on OpenWrt and other embedded systems where certbot is not available) cannot register or issue certificates against Vault's ACME server.

Environment

- Vault Server Version: 1.21.2 (likely affects other 1.21.x and possibly earlier)
- Tested from inside the Vault pod (direct HTTP to 127.0.0.1:8200) and from external client via HTTPS.

References

title: ACME new-nonce endpoint: Replay-Nonce header set in handler but missing from HTTP response
content:
References

- RFC 8555 §7.2 (newNonce): https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8555#section-7.2
- Related: issue #31536 (Replay-Nonce on badNonce error responses), which is a different but related compliance point.

title: [UI Regression] Kubernetes CA Certificate field rejects valid PEM certificates in v1.21.1+
content:
Describe the bug

After upgrading to Vault v1.21.1 (also confirmed on v1.21.2), the Kubernetes Auth Method configuration UI rejects valid PEM certificates with the error:

"The provided CA PEM data contains no valid certificates"

The same certificate works correctly when configured via CLI. This is a regression from previous versions where the UI accepted the certificate without issues.

To Reproduce
Upgrade to vault v1.21.1 or v1.21.2

title: [UI Regression] Kubernetes CA Certificate field rejects valid PEM certificates in v1.21.1+
content:
To Reproduce
Upgrade to vault v1.21.1 or v1.21.2

1. Enable Kubernetes auth method
2. Navigate to: Access → Auth Methods → [kubernetes-auth] → Configuration → Configure
3. Paste a valid PEM certificate into the "Kubernetes CA Certificate" field , add Kubernetes host.
4. Click Save

title: Avoid poisoning the trusted certificate cache on error
content:
Description
This change makes the trusted certificate cache retry (with exponential backoff) in the face of storage failures. 
Fixes #31348.

Background

title: Avoid poisoning the trusted certificate cache on error
content:
Fixes #31348.

Background
The code that loads the trusted certificate cache for cert-based authentication ignores any error that occurs while attempting to load any of the certificates that it finds. Undoubtedly some deployments have broken certificates or other non-certificate files stored in their respective back-ends, and so this is important behavior: we don't want to fail authentication just because is not a valid certificate!

title: Avoid poisoning the trusted certificate cache on error
content: In addition, because listing files and loading certificates is expensive, the server maintains a cache of trusted certificates. This cache is populated the first time it's needed, and then used for the lifetime of the process. If a file fails to load as a certificate, then it is simply not included in the cache.

title: Avoid poisoning the trusted certificate cache on error
content:
These two things lead to a problem when using a backend that might be subject to transient failures: a hiccough in the certificate loading process can cause the server to establish a cache that is missing an otherwise valid certificate. This can then lead to clients failing to authenticate to the server, until such time as the server is restarted and the cache reloaded.

Fix

title: Avoid poisoning the trusted certificate cache on error
content:
Fix
This change makes the certificate cache more resilient to loading failures, by caching partial successes. With this patch, the cache behavior becomes:

- If the cache exists and is either complete or it is not yet time to attempt to reload the certificates, then the cached results are used without reservation.

- Otherwise we attempt to load the certificates from storage:

 - If the cache does not already exist then a new, empty cache is created.

title: Avoid poisoning the trusted certificate cache on error
content:
- The storage is listed, we attempt to load everything in storage, skipping things that we have already successfully loaded, and skipping things that we cannot load, as usual.

 - Once we have attempted to load everything from storage, if there were any errors, we compute a deadline for retrying the load, with an exponentially increasing delay. If there were no errors, then the cache is considered complete, and there will be no retry.

title: Avoid poisoning the trusted certificate cache on error
content:
This has the nice behavior that we recover from transient failures eventually, while the exponential back-off ensures that we don't waste too much time attempting to load certificates that can never be loaded.

title: Vault uses Let's Encrypt's Private Enterprise Number
content:
Describe the bug
The following configuration example and test use unallocated OIDs from our Private Enterprise Number in tests:

builtin/logical/pki/path_roles.go
builtin/logical/pki/pathrolestest.go

Many people appear to have copy-pasted values from Let's Encrypt certificates without understanding the consequences of this, and as a result it is challenging for us (Let's Encrypt) to find where people are relying on specifics of our certificates.

title: Vault uses Let's Encrypt's Private Enterprise Number
content:
I recognize that this is only in a test/example and so may not feel like the biggest consequence, but the OID is going to be used as the Trust Anchor ID for a root we've just generated, so having it show up in Vault (and the many forks, etc) is a hindrance to us tracking genuine references to that root.

To Reproduce
go test ./...

Expected behavior
RFC 5612 allocated a Private Enterprise Number, 32473, for use in documentation, examples, etc.

title: Vault uses Let's Encrypt's Private Enterprise Number
content:
I would very much appreciate it if you could use 32473 instead of 44947 in your tests and example configuration.

title: Vault OIDC flow breaks fails to prompt users to login when their client tokens have expired
content:
Describe the bug

In older versions of vault a user would be prompted to log back into Vault when their Web UI client token had expired.

Newer versions of vault do not prompt users to log back in, instead they just send an error message back to the oauth client that initiated the OIDC auth flow.

This is annoying because I have to tell those using our vault deployment that they need to go and manually log into vault before they can use any application we have OIDC enabled on.

title: Vault OIDC flow breaks fails to prompt users to login when their client tokens have expired
content:
To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:

Run two versions of vault:

1. a version of vault where this was not an issue: 1.15.6
2. a recent version of vault where OIDC flows are busted: 1.17.2 (I think it broke in 1.16)

I have an example docker-compose.yml that I have been using to reproduce this issue

I also have a terraform file (main.tf) that I use to quickly configure the dev vault instances in a similar way.

This terraform state does the following:

title: Vault OIDC flow breaks fails to prompt users to login when their client tokens have expired
content:
This terraform state does the following:

1. setup a vault user + userpass
2. configure vault to allow CORS from an OIDC Debugging service (https://oidcdebugger.com/)
3. add a public oidc client for the OIDC debugger

For my testing I do the following:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

I then open two tabs of https://oidcdebugger.com/ and fill out the required information to test out the OIDC provider feature of Vault.

I then login to the vault ui for both versions using the test creds

title: Vault OIDC flow breaks fails to prompt users to login when their client tokens have expired
content:
I then login to the vault ui for both versions using the test creds 

- working older vault
- broken newer vault

and manually revoke their web ui client tokens (this is so I don't need to wait for them to naturally expire)

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

I then start the OIDC flow in both of my OIDC debugger tabs.

title: Vault OIDC flow breaks fails to prompt users to login when their client tokens have expired
content:
I then start the OIDC flow in both of my OIDC debugger tabs.

You'll see that the older version of vault will prompt you to login again, and if you enter the test creds again vault will complete the OIDC flow and the OIDC debugger will be happy.

In the modern version of Vault the vault ui will flash by in an instant as you are imidetily redirected back to the OIDC debugger with an error saying your client token is not valid.

Expected behavior

title: Vault OIDC flow breaks fails to prompt users to login when their client tokens have expired
content:
Expected behavior
Vault should prompt the user to re authenticate to vault when an OIDC client redirects a user to Vault to complete an auth flow and their vault web ui client token has expired like it did in previous versions of Vault.

Environment:
* Vault Server Version (retrieve with ): 1.17.2

* Server Operating System/Architecture: Linux

 is enough to showcase this issue.

title: 1.19.0 Breaking Bug: AWS STS 400 Error: {"errors":["number of regions does not match number of endpoints"]}
content:
Describe the bug
After updating to 1.19.0, when calling an aws sts endpoint, we now receive . These calls all used to work just fine. 

The new error was introduced in this PR https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/pull/29051

The AWS backend is configured like:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: 1.19.0 Breaking Bug: AWS STS 400 Error: {"errors":["number of regions does not match number of endpoints"]}
content:
The AWS backend is configured like:

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

You will note that in this config, no sts endpoint is set at all since that is supposed to be an optional parameter, so it was previously using whatever the default was. If I'm following the flow correctly, was likely nil at this point https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/blob/v1.18.5/builtin/logical/aws/client.go#L96 and AWS SDK probably had a fallback.

On the new PR / 1.19.0:

title: 1.19.0 Breaking Bug: AWS STS 400 Error: {"errors":["number of regions does not match number of endpoints"]}
content:
On the new PR / 1.19.0:

fallback region is specifically set to us-east-1 in the client.go file https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/blob/c937b3e2546bb40606b4cba1d1a5a1a53bfc2840/builtin/logical/aws/client.go#L36 and then later, if no region(s) were otherwise set, this hardcoded default is added to the regions list https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/blob/c937b3e2546bb40606b4cba1d1a5a1a53bfc2840/builtin/logical/aws/client.go#L96

title: 1.19.0 Breaking Bug: AWS STS 400 Error: {"errors":["number of regions does not match number of endpoints"]}
content:
No such logic exists for endpoints - if no endpoints are set in config at all (which is not documented as required), the endpoints will be an empty list.

The combination of these two factors results in this error always firing for very simple sts configurations, since endpoints will be 0 and regions will always be at least 1. (I have not tried editing the code and trying a new build, but this is the only explanation I see at first glance that explains the issue)

title: Directly exec ExternalTokenHelper rather than using a SHELL
content:
Description
What does this PR do?

Should resolve #29417 

There are many ways using a shell to exec the sub-command can go wrong. Whether that is unexpected spaces in the command or that the shell reads config files that output unexpected values. We encountered the unexpected output due to a very strange interaction with vault being used in a shell. We ran into this using GNU bash, version 5.0.17(1)-release (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu) in a pipe over an ssh connection.

title: Directly exec ExternalTokenHelper rather than using a SHELL
content: This stemmed from folks setting a default export VAULTADDR=https://vault.example.com in their ~/.bashrc, and that somehow when compiled with SSHSOURCEBASHRC defined as is apparently the default in some linux distributions, when bash detects it is used in a pipe and that variable is set (as it is when you have ssh'd to a remote host), bash will source ~/.bashrc, overriding the desired VAULTADDR. This would happen, for example, with vault list -address https://test-vault.example.com foo | cat

title: Directly exec ExternalTokenHelper rather than using a SHELL
content: . This fails to login because the tokenhelper returns the token for vault.example.com instead of test-vault.example.com. It is also an issue with updating the VAULTADDR in your environment to something other than the default before executing vault.

title: Directly exec ExternalTokenHelper rather than using a SHELL
content:
Because there was already a requirement that the path was a fully qualified path without any extra flags, it should be safe to directly exec the helper. This avoids any surprise interactions with the environment or with shell startup scripts.

title: Possible Data Race in core.go
content:
Bug Description

In file: , there is a possible case of data race. In line 2100, variable is written to without any kind of locking mechanism. And in line 2088, function call starts a call chain that leads to starting a goroutine with function . The new goroutine eventually invokes the function which reads the same variable using a read lock .

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: Possible Data Race in core.go
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Since two separate goroutines access the same memory location without proper synchronization, it will cause a data race condition.

To Reproduce

The bug was detected using static analysis. We did not reproduce the bug by executing source code.

Expected behavior

The bug was detected using static analysis, Here expected behavior would be accessing the shared variable with proper locking mechanism.

The Vault audit log fields do not correlate to passed that are set in headers by many typical Load-Balancer. There is also no other IP related field in the audit logs to determine consumer clients which may be important to overall efficacy of audits.

title: Vault Audit Log no correlation to X-Forwarded-For, Forwarded & remote_address static

title: Vault Audit Log no correlation to X-Forwarded-For, Forwarded & remote_address static
content: Support standards of preceding , and proceeding (rfc7239) in Vault Audit Logs

title: Vault Audit Log no correlation to X-Forwarded-For, Forwarded & remote_address static
content: . These may be best offered as new / optional fields that the user can set as required with consideration for privacy being noted as part of the process and what's more it may be a good idea to offer a feature to mask all Public IP addresses (IPv4 & IPv6); simply put providing a feature to mask the 2x inner bytes of any IPv4 Public address can help where the available information may still be used to further scope available logs elsewhere (on other devices)

title: Vault Audit Log no correlation to X-Forwarded-For, Forwarded & remote_address static
content: . There are also cases where these concerns of privacy are not applicable (resolved on other layers of the network) and when there's a need for Vault to log all IP's as is.

title: Vault agent randomly fails to authenticate
content:
We have vault set up on 2 AWS k8s clusters and both are exhibiting the same behaviour.

When a pod is deployed on to a AWS K8s Spot instance the vault agent randomly times out authenticating and remains like that.
On deleting the pod the Vault agent works as expected.

I'm fairly convinced this happens when a new node is spun up because of lack of capacity but as it happens randomly I can't be certain.

title: Vault agent randomly fails to authenticate

This can happen randomly and we have the same behaviour across 2 clusters. One is running v1.17.2, the other v1.18.1.

We didn't see this behaviour until we introduced Network policies but the policies include allowing egress for port 8200 and vault currently has no Network policy assigned.

title: VAULT-6803: fix listener issue if using `proxy_protocol_behavior` with `deny_unauthorized` for untrusted upstream connections
content:
Description

This PR corrects an issue introduced when supported was added for on TCP listeners using .

This issue was introduced in Vault and has impacted all subsequent versions other than (due to a bug parsing this config setting).

Any listener configured to will still close a connection when the upstream proxy is untrusted, but it will now continue to listen for connections until a valid one is found. It does this by returning to the proxy library.

title: `VAULT_AGENT_ADDR` has higher precedence than `-agent-address`

and proceeds to (correctly) prioritize flag values (as well as "agent trumps server")

So before spending time trying to figure that out, I figured I'd wait for confirmation that it is indeed a bug and that it should be fixed. As well, the conversation on the PR that introduced these changes made it seem like "agent wins over server" had some nuances and that maybe this 'bug' isn't as straightforward as I thought, and maybe you want to handle it yourselves.

title: lock public JWKS generation and re-check cache
content:
Description
What does this PR do?

This adds a new lock that just guards the public JWKS generation to ensure multiple requests aren't generating it at the same time. It is only used by the public JWKS endpoint and the token introspection endpoint. After acquiring the lock it double checks the cache to avoid expiring the keys again.

title: lock public JWKS generation and re-check cache
content: Currently, if multiple concurrent JWKS requests hit vault at the same time and there is no cached or values in the cache all the requests will proceed to this lock and unnecessarily (after the first request) re-expire the JWKS. This causes unnecessary load on the CPU and storage backend. Expiring the JWKS can also flush the cache which results in more misses and expiration calls, making the problem worse.

title: lock public JWKS generation and re-check cache
content: We have observed this behavior multiple times in our vault clusters with the latest instance resulting in hundreds of thousands of backlogged requests for JWKS (most of which were cancelled on the client side but were still being processed by vault). This backlog of requests took over 8 hours to process with the CPU maxed out on a 96 core EC2 instance. Below is the goroutine dump, goroutine count, instance CPU usage, and storage requests we observed.

title: Vault blocks (in certain conditions) even when one of two audit devices is available and working
content: Describe the bug

title: Vault blocks (in certain conditions) even when one of two audit devices is available and working
content: I have a Vault 0.11.x installed with two audit devices configured: one file device and one socket (TCP) device. I've noticed that if the TCP listener that is the target of the socket device is not listening and immediately returns an RST or an ICMP unreachable then everything is fine (i.e. since Vault can still write to the file device Vault operations proceed normally)

title: Vault blocks (in certain conditions) even when one of two audit devices is available and working
content: . But if the port is blocked by a firewall (for example) and silently ignores/drops the socket device request, then the socket device keeps on trying to contact the target eventually timing out, all the while "freezing" the Vault operation from the user's point of view, making it impossible to perform a Vault operation, even if the file device is up and logging properly.

title: Vault blocks (in certain conditions) even when one of two audit devices is available and working
content:
The docs for Blocked Audit Devices (https://www.vaultproject.io/docs/audit/index.html#blocked-audit-devices) state that "If you have more than one audit device, then Vault will complete the request as long as one audit device persists the log." This does not seem to be the case here.

Is this a bug, or does this statement not apply when the target audit device does not return any response (positive or negative) in a timely fashion?

To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:

Batch token after login before

title: identitystore entity leak causes major performance issues
content:
Describe the bug
The identitystore entities only grow and never shrink, causing long failover times (more than 10 minutes) due to entity loading and a storagepacker bottleneck during authentication. Additionally it causes high memory usage (increasing to 110GB when we list entitites).

We currently have over 5.7 million entities and growing:
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: identitystore entity leak causes major performance issues
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

This means each of the 256 storagepacker buckets that contains them each have around 22.3k entities, and each time a login occurs CreateOrFetchEntity() is called, it reads a storagepacker bucket, decompresses it, unmarshalls the protobuf, modifies it, marshals it, compresses it, and writes the bucket back. As a result we see Vault fail at about ~500 GCP auth requests per MINUTE.

title: identitystore entity leak causes major performance issues
content:
This also caused a Consul storage migration to fail due to the large key sizes of the storagepacker buckets.


To Reproduce
Authenticate millions of times with unique identities.

Expected behavior
I expect entities to be maintained to be a reasonable size.

Environment:
* Vault Server Version (retrieve with ):

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

* Vault CLI Version (retrieve with ):
 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
* Server Operating System/Architecture:
Linux, AMD64

content:
* Server Operating System/Architecture:
Linux, AMD64

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Additional context
We've been fighting these perf issues for over a year.

It seems the only place Vault deletes any dangling entities from storagepacker are entities and group entitites if the namespace is nil: 
https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/blob/rel-1.4.0/vault/identitystoreutil.go#L112

title: Vault Agent - Self-DDOS due to failure on rendering 1 template causing all templates to be rerendered
content:
Describe the bug
We have Vault Agent configured to render multiple templates, with each template retrieving certain secrets from Vault using the function. If one template fails to retrieve a secret (perhaps due to a permissions/policy issues), it triggers a retry. At the end of the retry, the runner restarts, forcing all secrets for all templates to be retrieved again and rerendered. This goes into a loop, which effective DDoses our Vault server.

To Reproduce

title: Race condition in OIDC auth in browser
content:
Describe the bug
There is a really nasty issue in OIDC authentication that bugged me for quite some time. Sometimes I had to retry "Sign in with OIDC provider" like tens of times to succeed. The problem reproduced only in Firefox and only on my laptop. However, the experience was maddening so I had to figure it out.

It seems like there is a race between the window with button "Sign in with OIDC provider" and the popup window, that does second part of authentication.

To Reproduce

title: Race condition in OIDC auth in browser
content:
To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Use Firefox. Set to 100000 ms in about:config to make the issue 100% reproducible (default value is 1000 ms).
2. Open sign in page (Vault should be set up with OIDC auth)
3. Press "Sign in with OIDC provider"
4. Wait a little bit
5. See the error "The provider window was closed before authentication was complete. Please click Sign In to try again".

title: Race condition in OIDC auth in browser
content:
On my particular hardware+software setup on the default Firefox settings the reproducibility is in the ballpark of 90%.

Expected behavior
Sign in should succeed.

Environment:
* Vault Server Version (retrieve with ): 1.12.2
* Vault CLI Version (retrieve with ): Vault v1.15.6 (cgo) (not needed to reproduce the issue)
* Server Operating System/Architecture: Manjaro Linux 23.1.3
* Firefox: 124.0

Additional context

title: Race condition in OIDC auth in browser
content:
* Firefox: 124.0

Additional context
I believe there is some kind of sleep in JavaScript in the window with the "Sign in with OIDC provider" button. This sleep is being throttled when the window goes to background. This results in waking up too late, when the popup window is already closed.

If I manually switch focus from the popup window to the main one, making it foreground, the issue doesn't reproduce.

title: Make lifetime_watcher Cache-Control Age header aware.
content:
What
https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/issues/16439 identified that occasionally the Vault Agent Template server would wake up at the incorrect time to renew leased credentials.


I believe I've identified a potential root cause which is that the Vault API library was not accounting for the header returned from the cache. Without accounting for cache template servers will work from the original TTL from the cached renew request instead of the true aged TTL.

title: Make lifetime_watcher Cache-Control Age header aware.
content:
The design and reasoning for this current approach is explained in more detail here https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/issues/16439#issuecomment-1230233485

How
1. API library for Sys.Renew() and Auth().Token().RenewTokenAsSelf have methods that return the full response including the header. 22cc6feba9a12844ca5d182dd6d5c9913abd704d
2. The Vault Agent now updates the when forwarding cached responses be0b314f81876969ce01bf86f2a71a64ec280f14

title: Make lifetime_watcher Cache-Control Age header aware.
content:
3. lifetime_watcher becomes Age aware cb0b92c9d7b6767d920ecb194789d0a1d0f761a3

We need to write tests and run tests.


Why
The Vault Agent Cache inserts an header based on the time the original response was received from the Vault Server (source).

The lifetime_watcher needs access to the Age header, as opposed to modifying the TTL, because the TTL has a very strong affect on the sleep duration. This is because the TTL is used in multiple terms of the sleep duration.

title: Make lifetime_watcher Cache-Control Age header aware.
content:
To achieve the correct sleepDuration the Age must be subtracted from the sleepDuration after all other calculations.



fixes: https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/issues/16439

title: Do not generate HMAC keys for CMAC keys on calls to Upgrade
content:
- Missed during the initial development of the Transit CMAC feature, on initial key creation we did not generate HMAC keys when the key type was CMAC. The call to the policy's Upgrade function though would treat this key as requiring an upgrade and add one back.
 - Fix this by adding an HMACSupported argument and verifying on upgrade for HMAC creation that the key type supports HMAC
 - Add generic test that verifies we aren't changing a key type iota value, string it defined and the proper operation of HMACSupported and CMACSupported functions

title: UI Remove recent instances of sessionStorage
content:
This PR removes remaining references to sessionStorage. Here's what happened:

1. We swapped localStorage in favor of sessionStorage in #14054 
2. OIDC bugfix in #14545 used sessionStorage because that's what was standard at the time
3. Team realized the move to sessionStorage was a mistake, and reverted 14054 in #15769 
4. Changes from 14545 are still present in 1.11 onward, and this PR is to fix that.

title: Fix exponential backoff for api.LifetimeWatcher
content:
I just opened up a bug for this PR, this fixes it. Here's the original context from the bug:

Describe the bug
We were using the Vault from the package in an internal project and noticed an issue with the backoff behavior of token renewal that was causing a bunch of our tests to fail when we upgraded to a new version of Vault.

The bug is here:

https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/blob/1274f2d79a1ae6f589ad64af2c1ab930545943c0/api/lifetime_watcher.go#L348-L354

title: Fix exponential backoff for api.LifetimeWatcher
content:
appears to be the used prior to re-running the renewal loop. In the case when is , then a simple backoff duration is calculated based on the call to . If is not nil then is never set and the timeout in the following select block immediately fires again.

In our testing environment this was caught because our mock Vault server was returning an invalid response, so the renew operation was failing and we were getting an inordinate amount of immediate retries.

title: Fix exponential backoff for api.LifetimeWatcher
content:
The fix is just refactoring the above block to capture the value as .

fixes: https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/issues/26382

title: Validate OCSP response is signed by expected issuer
content:
- There was a bug in the OCSP response signature logic, it properly verified but kept around the ocspRes object around so we ignored the errors found and passed the response object back up the stack.
 - Now extract the verification logic into a dedicated function, if it returns an error, blank the ocspRes response as we can't trust it.
 - Address an issue that the OCSP requests from multiple servers were clobbering each others responses as the index loop variable was not properly captured.
 - Add a missing validation that the response was for the serial number we requested

title: Address OCSP client caching issue
content:
- The OCSP cache built into the client that is used by cert-auth would cache the responses but when pulling out a cached value the response wasn't validating properly and was then thrown away. So effectively we were operating with no cache.

 - The issue was around a confusion of the client's internal status vs the Go SDK OCSP status integer values.

title: Vault agent DoS's the vault server if template command fails
content:
Describe the bug
Running a vault agent with a command that returns a non-zero error code results in the template being restarted immediately with no back-off or retry delay. This results in many requests to the Vault server in quick succession which can cause a Vault outage due to resource exhaustion.

To Reproduce
1. Setup Vault agent with a template stanza containing a command that fails
2. Watch the Vault Agent logs to see it repeatedly restarting the template without any back-off

title: Vault agent DoS's the vault server if template command fails
content:
3. Look at the Vault Server logs and observe that not only is it wasting local resources, it's also wasting resources on the Vault server too

Expected behavior
Other areas of the Vault Agent such as the auto auth implement a back-off mechanism on failure to avoid excessive resource consumption (either locally or from the Vault server). A similar back-off when restarting the template would resolve this issue.

Environment:
Vault v1.8.2 (aca76f63357041a43b49f3e8c11d67358496959f)

title: Vault agent DoS's the vault server if template command fails

#9059 may or may not be related to this, I'm not sure enough of consul-templates internals to know if setting that retry would fix this issue or not.

title: api/client.go#clone() re-claims a non-re-entrant lock
content:
Describe the bug
In api/client.go, clone() claims the same read lock twice. The second time is via Headers().

The doc on RWMutex says in here and here that re-entrance is not allowed.

We ran into this problem when a goroutine called CloneWithHeaders() and another called SetCloneToken().
After CloneWithHeaders() acquired the first read lock, SetCloneToken() tried to claim a write lock. As a result,

title: api/client.go#clone() re-claims a non-re-entrant lock
content:
no new read lock could be claimed and goroutine one couldn't release the existing read lock. As a result,
goroutine two couldn't acquire the write lock. Below are the stacktraces.

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:

To reproduce this issue, you need to have a small piece of code that has the following operations.
We had them back-to-back.

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED]

title: api/client.go#clone() re-claims a non-re-entrant lock
content:
We had them back-to-back.

 [CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 

Then you need to execute this small piece of code with multiple threads non-stop.
You will see the problem if you are (un)lucky enough.

Note that regardless of the reproduction, please consider Go's documentation on RWMutex.

We use pprof to spy on the goroutines.

Expected behavior
No deadlocking.

Environment:
* Vault Server Version (retrieve with ): 1.12.0
* Vault CLI Version (retrieve with ): v1.8.3

n/a

Additional context
A problem I can see that makes client.go risky is that top-level functions can invoke each other.
I think if you structure the code so that only top-level functions can claim locks and that top-level
functions cannot invoke each other, then it's unlikely for lock-related issues to take place.

title: api/client.go#clone() re-claims a non-re-entrant lock
content: See this post on why golang doesn't offer re-entrant locks in the first place.

title: Bug fix: Update KV data when you change the version of a nested secret
content:
Fixes #25029.

Regression caused by #24224

This was caused because we were using a tracked property ( ) for the secret data and we needed a getter that relied on the model arg. The secret model was updating and thus the was correct behind the scenes but the tracked property was not re-firing because it didn't rely on anything that was triggering a re-fire of the property. 

- [x] Enterprise tests pass. I realize this isn't an enterprise area, but being conservative on all things KV.
- [x] Make sure new test coverage fails without the changes (suggestion by Claire, thank you).

title: UI: Database fixes
content:
This PR adds long-overdue workflow tests with mirage backend for database secrets engine. Through writing the tests, I found a couple bugs that are fixed with these changes. 

Found bugs
- attempting to save a connection, save fails, and then updating the connection name causes an ember-data error
- Adding a role does not update the connection with the updated role automatically as intended
- After creating a connection, the transition to the details page for the connection fails

title: Backport of Fixed Oauth redirect not working on Android Chrome into release/1.13.x
content:
This fixes the issue described in https://github.com/hashicorp/vault/issues/16778. Navigation is blocked in Android chrome while redirecting back after OIDC authentication. The issue is explained by the lead maintainer of
AppAuth(https://stackoverflow.com/a/41882732).
The latest Chrome version redirects to the app only if triggered by the user and not automatically redirect. Hence, a link is added in the UI to redirect back to the app.

title: UI: fix PKI issuer capabilities
content: There was a bug where capabilities on PKI issuers were incorrectly calculated. The workflow tests did not catch this because we weren't clearing the store of entries after setup, which means the UI was using the old capabilities in memory from the root setup.

title: UI: better calculation for advanced secret in KV v2
content:
In #24308 we inadvertently introduced an issue where secrets with values that include would be locked to the JSON view. This PR makes the check in a more intelligent way so that the original intended behavior is preserved, but secret values are not shown. 

Now, string values with are shown as inputs instead of with the JSON editor: 
 
 

Out of scope for this backport but as a future improvement, we will add obscurity to the JSON view that is toggleable

title: UI: context menu for a KV secret, fails on non-toplevel paths
content:
Describe the bug
When clicking on the context menu (three dots) for a secret not located at the toplevel, the context menu pops up but ends up showing only Details, even if the user has delete permissions.

This is caused by the UI making a wrong call to the endpoint.

To Reproduce

1. Start a Vault Dev server.
2. Store a secret in a subpath in the KV store: 
3. Create a policy granting full access to the subpath, listing the toplevel, and access to the endpoint:

title: UI: context menu for a KV secret, fails on non-toplevel paths
content:
[CODE BLOCK REMOVED] 
4. Create a token having this policy: 
5. Log in to the UI using the token, navigate to secret/subpath.
6. Use the dev tools in your browser to inspect the request that the UI makes to the endpoint when you click on the three dots beside the secret located in .
7. You will notice that the browser makes a request with the wrong path, and gets a in the response, which is expected.




Expected behavior

content:
Expected behavior
When the user has the necessary permissions on a secret, the context menu should show all options.
The UI should POST the full path to the endpoint:
